Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC10980.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule shows significant inconsistency, particularly on the weekends. Your overall bedtime standard deviation is 01:22 (66th percentile), but this jumps to a highly variable 02:01 (87th percentile) on weekends. Your wake time standard deviation is 01:09 (59th percentile), and your midsleep point standard deviation is 01:00 (62nd percentile), indicating a fluctuating sleep routine overall.

### Sleep Quality
Your restorative sleep stages are on the lower end, with average deep sleep at 00:45 (21st percentile) and REM sleep at 01:01 (26th percentile). Despite this, your sleep is remarkably undisturbed; your restlessness score is very low at 0.057 (11th percentile). Your overall sleep score is 78.85 (54th percentile), though you score slightly better on weekends (81.26, 70th percentile) than on workdays (77.77, 45th percentile). 

### Alertness
You recorded 2 naps during this period. Your average nap duration is quite long at 69.5 minutes (65th percentile). Naps exceeding 30 minutes can sometimes cause sleep inertia or interfere with your nighttime sleep drive.

### Timing
Your sleep timing is significantly earlier than most similar users. Your average bedtime is 21:50 (20th percentile), and your average wake time is 05:04 (8th percentile). This early pattern is even more pronounced on workdays, with an average bedtime of 21:37 (16th percentile) and a wake time of 04:48 (6th percentile). Consequently, your average midsleep point is very early at 01:27 (11th percentile).

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is a strong point. You have an average sleep efficiency of 88.2% (70th percentile), meaning you spend the vast majority of your time in bed actually sleeping. You also spend very little time awake during the night, with an average wake time after sleep onset of just 10.97 minutes (18th percentile). Your time to quality sleep is average at 21 minutes (56th percentile).

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 06:22, which is relatively short and places you in the 26th percentile compared to similar users. You sleep slightly longer on weekends (06:32, 31st percentile) compared to workdays (06:17, 27th percentile). Additionally, your sleep duration standard deviation is 01:22 (67th percentile), showing that the total amount of sleep you get varies considerably from night to night.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC12706.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly inconsistent, particularly on workdays. Your overall bedtime standard deviation is 1 hour and 42 minutes (83rd percentile), and this variability increases to 1 hour and 53 minutes on workdays (88th percentile). Consequently, your midsleep point standard deviation is also elevated at 1 hour and 8 minutes (76th percentile). Your wake time fluctuates moderately, with a standard deviation of 1 hour and 8 minutes (61st percentile).

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep quality is notably poor. Your average sleep score is 71.01, placing you in the bottom 9th percentile, and drops even lower on workdays to a median score of 70.40 (7th percentile). This low score is heavily driven by a lack of restorative sleep stages: your deep sleep averages only 39 minutes (23rd percentile) and your REM sleep averages just 47 minutes (19th percentile). Additionally, your time to quality sleep is prolonged at 29 minutes (90th percentile). 

### Alertness
You do not take any naps, placing your nap frequency and average nap length of 0.0 minutes in the 0th percentile. 

### Timing
Your sleep timing leans slightly later than average. Your average bedtime is 23:35 (75th percentile), and your average midsleep point is 03:07 (69th percentile). Despite the late bedtimes, your average wake time is 06:40 (52nd percentile), which remains exactly the same on average across both workdays and weekends. 

### Efficiency
Your overall sleep efficiency is 87.1%, which is average (57th percentile) compared to similar users. Once you fall asleep, you do a good job of staying asleep, with an average wake time after sleep onset of only 11.79 minutes (26th percentile). However, as noted, your time to quality sleep is unusually high at 29 minutes (90th percentile), meaning it takes you much longer than your peers to transition into deeper, restorative sleep stages.

### Duration
Your sleep duration is shorter than ideal and highly erratic. You average 6 hours and 10 minutes of sleep per night (27th percentile). This drops to 6 hours and 5 minutes on workdays (26th percentile) and only slightly recovers to 6 hours and 24 minutes on weekends (31st percentile). Furthermore, your sleep duration standard deviation is 1 hour and 33 minutes (85th percentile), indicating significant day-to-day instability in how much rest you actually get.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC15677.insights_output:
# Sleep Insights Report

### Routine
*   Your bedtime is highly inconsistent, with a standard deviation of 1 hour and 29 minutes, placing you in the 80th percentile. 
*   Your midsleep point also shows significant irregularity, with a standard deviation of 1 hour and 1 minute (72nd percentile).
*   Your wake time is relatively more stable, showing a standard deviation of 56 minutes (50th percentile).

### Sleep Quality
*   Your overall average sleep score is 67.41, which is exceptionally low and places you in the 3rd percentile.
*   Your deep sleep duration is critically low at an average of just 20 minutes (1st percentile).
*   Your REM sleep duration is also severely reduced, averaging only 30 minutes (4th percentile).
*   Your light sleep duration is normal, averaging 5 hours and 6 minutes (59th percentile).
*   Your restlessness score is 0.095, which is average (56th percentile).
*   Your time to quality sleep is 15 minutes (39th percentile), and your wake time after sleep onset averages 16.54 minutes (72nd percentile).

### Alertness
*   You recorded 2 naps during this period.
*   Your average nap length is 96 minutes, which is unusually long and places you in the 92nd percentile. 

### Timing
*   Your average bedtime is 23:26 (72nd percentile). However, your timing shifts significantly later on the weekend, with an average weekend bedtime of 00:27 (90th percentile) compared to 23:00 on workdays (60th percentile).
*   Your average wake time is 06:29 (47th percentile). This shifts from 06:19 on workdays (44th percentile) to 06:54 on weekends (53rd percentile).
*   Your average midsleep point is 02:58 (61st percentile), but it is pushed nearly an hour later on weekends to 03:40 (79th percentile) compared to 02:40 on workdays (51st percentile).

### Efficiency
*   Your average sleep efficiency is 84.7%, which is poor (15th percentile). This sits very close to the bottom 5th percentile of similar users (83.4%).
*   Your wake time after sleep onset (16.54 minutes, 72nd percentile) is higher than average, though it remains below the top 95th percentile of similar users (20.68 minutes).
*   Your average time to quality sleep is 15 minutes (39th percentile), which is healthy and sits comfortably within the normal range of similar users (between 8 and 31 minutes).

### Duration
*   Your average sleep duration is 5 hours and 57 minutes, which is short and places you in the 19th percentile.
*   Your sleep duration drops severely on the weekend to just 5 hours and 24 minutes (6th percentile), which is dangerously close to the bottom 5th percentile of similar users (5 hours and 15 minutes). 
*   On workdays, your sleep duration is slightly better at 6 hours and 11 minutes (28th percentile).
*   Your sleep duration standard deviation is 1 hour and 10 minutes (62nd percentile), indicating moderate-to-high variability in how long you sleep from night to night.

Make sure to consider various sleep health dimensions: Routine, Sleep Quality, Alertness, Timing, Efficiency, and Duration. Add a heading for each dimension. Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC15690.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your bedtime consistency is excellent. Your bedtime standard deviation is 43 minutes, placing you in the 21st percentile, meaning you adhere to a stricter schedule than most similar users. Your midsleep point consistency is also strong (34th percentile), indicating a stable circadian rhythm.

### Sleep Quality
Your sleep architecture shows an imbalance toward lighter stages. Your deep sleep duration averages only 50 minutes, which is in the 26th percentile and on the lower end of the preferred range for physical restoration. Conversely, your light sleep is high, averaging 4 hours and 52 minutes (76th percentile). You also exhibit elevated restlessness (70th percentile), suggesting you move frequently during the night despite staying asleep.

### Alertness
Data indicates you do not rely on daytime sleep to function. You recorded only one nap during this period, though it was a significant duration of 60 minutes.

### Timing
Your sleep timing is socially aligned and normal for your demographic. Your average bedtime of 22:58 (59th percentile) and wake time of 06:48 (67th percentile) are standard. Notably, there is very little variation between your weekend and workday timing, which protects you against social jetlag.

### Efficiency
Your ability to maintain sleep is superior to most peers. Your sleep efficiency is 88.5% (76th percentile). You fall asleep quickly, with an average time to quality sleep of 16 minutes (35th percentile). Most impressively, your wake time after sleep onset is very low at roughly 9 minutes (14th percentile), indicating that once you fall asleep, you rarely spend significant time awake.

### Duration
You are achieving a healthy total sleep time. Your average sleep duration is 6 hours and 55 minutes, placing you in the 66th percentile. Your duration is also highly consistent day-to-day, with a standard deviation of only 52 minutes (26th percentile).

Identify all of the patterns of data that are likely out of the preferred range. Make sure to consider various sleep health dimensions: Routine, Sleep Quality, Alertness, Timing, Efficiency, and Duration. Add a heading for each dimension. Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. SC17450.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly irregular. Your bedtime standard deviation is 04:40 (98th percentile), your wake time standard deviation is 04:57 (98th percentile), and your midsleep point standard deviation is 04:42 (98th percentile). This indicates a severe lack of day-to-day consistency in your sleep routine.

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep quality is poor, reflected by an average sleep score of 72.85 (15th percentile). This is primarily driven by a severe lack of restorative sleep stages: your deep sleep averages just 00:30 (1st percentile), light sleep averages 03:32 (10th percentile), and REM sleep averages 00:55 (17th percentile). On a positive note, your restlessness score is average at 0.088 (48th percentile), your time to quality sleep is relatively quick at 15 minutes (30th percentile), and your wake time after sleep onset is extremely low at 7.10 minutes (3rd percentile). 

### Alertness
You recorded 4 naps, but your average nap length is exceptionally long at 146.75 minutes (98th percentile). Naps exceeding two hours strongly indicate excessive daytime sleepiness, likely a direct result of your poor nighttime sleep duration.

### Timing
Your sleep timing is significantly delayed compared to your peers. Your average bedtime is 01:31 (96th percentile), remaining consistently late across both workdays (01:27, 96th percentile) and weekends (01:42, 95th percentile). Because of these late bedtimes, your average midsleep point is pushed back to 04:19 (91st percentile). Your average wake time is 07:06 (73rd percentile), with weekend wake times (07:20, 55th percentile) being slightly later than workday wake times (06:59, 79th percentile).

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is a strong point, averaging 89.2% (87th percentile), which is very close to the top 95th percentile of similar users (89.8%). You spend almost no time awake in bed during the night; your wake time after sleep onset is just 7.10 minutes (3rd percentile), which is even lower than the bottom 5th percentile of your peers (7.62 minutes). Additionally, your time to quality sleep is 15 minutes (30th percentile), meaning you transition into restorative sleep faster than average.

### Duration
You are experiencing severe sleep deprivation. Your average sleep duration is only 04:59 (2nd percentile), which falls below the bottom 5th percentile of similar users (05:15). This extreme shortage is consistent throughout the week, with workday sleep averaging 04:57 (2nd percentile) and weekend sleep averaging 05:02 (3rd percentile). Furthermore, the amount of sleep you get fluctuates wildly from night to night, evidenced by a sleep duration standard deviation of 01:48 (93rd percentile).

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. SC18248.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your sleep schedule is exceptionally consistent, which is a major strength for your circadian rhythm.
*   **High Consistency:** Your bedtime consistency is in the **7th percentile** (standard deviation of 33 minutes) and your wake-time consistency is in the **8th percentile** (standard deviation of 37 minutes). This indicates you adhere to a strict schedule significantly better than most men your age.
*   **Stable Midpoint:** Your midsleep point deviation is in the **5th percentile**, showing that your internal body clock is very well-anchored.

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep structure is robust, particularly regarding physical restoration.
*   **Exceptional Deep Sleep:** You are averaging 1 hour and 23 minutes of deep sleep, placing you in the **92nd percentile**. This suggests excellent physical recovery and hormone regulation.
*   **Low Restlessness:** Your restlessness score is in the **16th percentile**, indicating you move significantly less than your peers and experience stable sleep continuity.
*   **Solid Continuity:** Your average wake time after sleep onset is only ~10 minutes ( **27th percentile**), meaning once you fall asleep, you stay asleep.
*   **Normal REM:** Your REM sleep duration (1 hour 11 minutes) is average (**48th percentile**), supporting standard cognitive processing and emotional regulation.

### Alertness
*   **Napping:** You rarely nap, with only one recorded instance. That single nap was 69 minutes long. While napping isn't a habit for you, be aware that naps exceeding 20-30 minutes can lead to sleep inertia (grogginess) upon waking.

### Timing
You tend to keep an earlier schedule than your peers, though you experience some "social jetlag" on weekends.
*   **Early Schedule:** Your average bedtime (22:20) is in the **30th percentile**, and your wake time (06:11) is in the **38th percentile**. You are naturally an earlier riser than the average user in your demographic.
*   **Weekend Shift:** While your bedtime remains consistent on weekends, your wake time shifts later by nearly 50 minutes (06:46 on weekends vs. 05:57 on workdays). This suggests you are using the weekend to pay off a mild sleep debt accumulated during the week.

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is high, indicating you spend most of your time in bed actually sleeping.
*   **High Efficiency:** With an average sleep efficiency of 88.4% (**72nd percentile**), you are utilizing your time in bed effectively.
*   **Fast Onset:** It takes you about 14 minutes to fall asleep (**22nd percentile**). This is a healthy latency, though falling asleep much faster could indicate exhaustion.

### Duration
You are getting more sleep than the majority of men in your age group, though your workday duration suggests a slight restriction.
*   **Above Average Total:** Your average sleep duration of 6 hours and 56 minutes is in the **71st percentile**.
*   **Workday vs. Weekend:** On workdays, you average 6 hours and 44 minutes (**67th percentile**), whereas on weekends you extend this to 7 hours and 24 minutes (**73rd percentile**). The increase on weekends confirms that your biological need is likely closer to 7.5 hours, and you are slightly restricting sleep during the workweek.

Identify all of the patterns of data that are likely out of the preferred range. Make sure to consider various sleep health dimensions: Routine, Sleep Quality, Alertness, Timing, Efficiency, and Duration. Add a heading for each dimension. Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC18524.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is critically low. You average **05:49** per night, placing you in the **13th percentile**. This restriction is most severe on workdays, where you average only **05:23** (7th percentile). While you extend your sleep to **06:53** on weekends (48th percentile), this "catch-up" pattern indicates a cycle of chronic sleep restriction during the week followed by recovery attempts on weekends.

### Timing
There is a significant discrepancy between your workday and weekend schedules, known as "social jetlag."
*   **Wake Times:** You wake up extremely early on workdays (**04:46**, 10th percentile) but shift significantly later on weekends (**07:09**, 43rd percentile). This variance of over 2 hours disrupts your circadian rhythm.
*   **Midsleep:** Your biological midpoint shifts from **01:43** on workdays to **03:12** on weekends.
*   **Bedtimes:** Your average workday bedtime is **22:41** (47th percentile), while your weekend bedtime is **23:15** (46th percentile). The issue lies in your wake times rather than your bedtimes.

### Routine
Despite the timing shift between workdays and weekends, you maintain high consistency within those specific categories.
*   **Weekend Consistency:** Your weekend bedtime standard deviation is extremely low at **21 minutes** (4th percentile), indicating you are very precise with when you go to bed on days off.
*   **Workday Consistency:** Your workday wake time standard deviation is **27 minutes** (21st percentile), showing a disciplined, albeit early, start to your work week.

### Sleep Quality
Your sleep consolidation is excellent, but your sleep stages suffer due to the short duration.
*   **Restlessness:** Your average restlessness score is **0.046**, placing you in the **0th percentile**. This is exceptional; once you are asleep, you hardly move.
*   **Sleep Score:** Consequently, your average sleep score is high at **81.1** (79th percentile).
*   **Sleep Stages:** Because your total time in bed is short, your absolute time in restorative stages is lower than preferred. Deep sleep averages **57 minutes** (29th percentile) and REM sleep averages **1 hour** (22nd percentile).

### Efficiency
You are a highly efficient sleeper.
*   **Sleep Efficiency:** Your average efficiency is **88.1%** (64th percentile).
*   **Wake After Sleep Onset (WASO):** You spend very little time awake after falling asleep, averaging only **9.83 minutes** (31st percentile).
*   **Latency:** Your time to quality sleep is normal at **19 minutes** (42nd percentile).

### Alertness
You recorded 3 naps with an average length of **73 minutes** (70th percentile). Naps of this duration (over an hour) are typically compensatory, further confirming that you are carrying a significant sleep debt from your work week that requires recovery.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC19241.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep routine shows significant inconsistency overall, driven almost entirely by your weekend habits. Your overall bedtime standard deviation is 02:26 (83rd percentile), your wake time standard deviation is 02:01 (76th percentile), and your midsleep point standard deviation is 02:07 (81st percentile). However, your workday schedule is highly consistent, with a bedtime standard deviation of just 00:40 (27th percentile) and a wake time standard deviation of 00:32 (19th percentile). On weekends, your routine breaks down completely: your bedtime standard deviation spikes to 04:01 (92nd percentile) and your wake time standard deviation rises to 02:22 (84th percentile).

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep quality is good, reflected by an average sleep score of 80 (72nd percentile). Your sleep architecture is well-balanced and normal, with light sleep at 04:11 (52nd percentile), deep sleep at 01:17 (57th percentile), and REM sleep at 01:14 (43rd percentile). You experience very little nighttime tossing and turning, with a low average restlessness score of 0.081 (27th percentile). 

### Alertness
You do not take any naps, placing both your total number of naps and average nap length in the 0th percentile.

### Timing
You exhibit a strong pattern of "social jetlag," shifting your sleep schedule significantly later on your days off. On workdays, your average bedtime is 22:52 (37th percentile) and your wake time is 06:06 (24th percentile). On weekends, your bedtime shifts nearly two hours later to 00:46 (63rd percentile), and your wake time shifts over three hours later to 09:14 (75th percentile). Consequently, your midsleep point shifts drastically from 02:29 (29th percentile) on workdays to 05:00 (71st percentile) on weekends.

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is healthy at 88% (65th percentile), sitting comfortably within the 84.4% to 89.9% range of similar users. You transition into quality sleep very quickly, taking only 15 minutes (14th percentile), which is near the bottom of the 13 to 37-minute range for your demographic. Your wake time after sleep onset is 11.32 minutes (53rd percentile), which is perfectly average compared to the 7.06 to 16.07-minute range of similar users.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 06:43 (50th percentile), sitting exactly in the middle of the 05:33 to 07:48 range for similar users. However, your sleep duration standard deviation is somewhat high at 01:20 (66th percentile) due to a noticeable discrepancy between workdays and weekends. You sleep significantly less on workdays (06:21, 34th percentile) and compensate by sleeping over an hour longer on weekends (07:30, 74th percentile). Both workday and weekend durations fall within the expected ranges for similar users, but the variation indicates you are using weekends to catch up on sleep debt accumulated during the week.

SC20094.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly inconsistent. Your overall bedtime standard deviation is 01:36 (77th percentile), which worsens significantly on weekends to 02:07 (85th percentile). Your wake time standard deviation is also highly variable at 01:38 (75th percentile), reaching 01:52 on weekends (81st percentile). Consequently, your midsleep point standard deviation is elevated at 01:23 (77th percentile). 

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep score is average at 78.81 (54th percentile). While your deep sleep duration is excellent at 01:20 (84th percentile), your light sleep duration is exceptionally low at 03:42 (8th percentile), and your REM sleep is on the lower end at 01:14 (32nd percentile). Additionally, your sleep is frequently disturbed, indicated by a high restlessness score of 0.100 (77th percentile). Your time to quality sleep is 00:21 (57th percentile), and your wake time after sleep onset is 10.47 minutes (34th percentile).

### Alertness
You recorded 0 naps, resulting in an average nap length of 0.0 minutes (0th percentile). 

### Timing
Your sleep timing shifts noticeably between workdays and weekends. On workdays, your average bedtime is 22:39 (56th percentile) and your wake time is 05:41 (27th percentile). On weekends, your bedtime shifts over an hour later to 23:51 (75th percentile), and your wake time shifts to 07:12 (41st percentile). This pushes your average midsleep point from 02:10 on workdays (41st percentile) to 03:31 on weekends (62nd percentile).

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is healthy at 88.4% (61st percentile) compared to similar users. You spend very little time awake during the night, with a wake time after sleep onset of just 10.47 minutes (34th percentile). Your time to quality sleep is average at 21 minutes (57th percentile), indicating normal sleep onset compared to your peers.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is significantly shorter than preferred at just 06:17 (17th percentile). This lack of sleep is consistent across the week, with workday sleep duration at 06:11 (19th percentile) and weekend sleep duration at 06:30 (18th percentile). Furthermore, the amount of sleep you get fluctuates heavily from night to night, with a sleep duration standard deviation of 01:30 (78th percentile).

Identify all of the patterns of data that are likely out of the preferred range. Make sure to consider various sleep health dimensions: Routine, Sleep Quality, Alertness, Timing, Efficiency, and Duration. Add a heading for each dimension. Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. SC20240.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your wake-up consistency on workdays is exceptional. With a standard deviation of only 15 minutes, your workday wake stability is in the **6th percentile**, indicating a much stricter routine than similar users. However, your bedtime consistency is more variable (59th percentile), fluctuating by over an hour on average.

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep health is excellent, with an average sleep score of 83.18, placing you in the **93rd percentile**.
*   **Light Sleep:** You spend a significant amount of time in light sleep (average 5 hours 25 minutes), placing you in the **93rd percentile**. This suggests your sleep architecture is heavily weighted toward lighter stages.
*   **REM & Deep Sleep:** Your REM sleep is healthy (71st percentile), and your Deep sleep is average (56th percentile).
*   **Restlessness:** Your restlessness levels are typical for your demographic (48th percentile).

### Alertness
You recorded zero naps during this period. Given your high nightly sleep duration, this suggests you are maintaining sufficient alertness throughout the day without requiring supplemental rest.

### Timing
You have an early chronotype compared to your peers.
*   **Bedtime:** Your average bedtime of 21:41 is in the **17th percentile** (earlier than 83% of users).
*   **Social Jetlag:** There is a noticeable shift in your schedule between workdays and weekends. Your average weekend bedtime shifts later by over an hour (22:28), and your weekend wake time shifts later by nearly 75 minutes (07:25).

### Efficiency
*   **Sleep Efficiency:** Your average efficiency is 86.8%, which is average (44th percentile).

Identify all of the patterns of data that are likely out of the preferred range. Make sure to consider various sleep health dimensions: Routine, Sleep Quality, Alertness, Timing, Efficiency, and Duration. Add a heading for each dimension. Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC23912.insights_output:
### Routine
*   Your bedtime consistency is highly variable, with a bedtime standard deviation of 01:38 (75th percentile). This inconsistency is present on both workdays (79th percentile) and weekends (81st percentile).
*   Your wake time standard deviation is 01:27 (68th percentile). However, your routine is much more consistent on workdays, where your wake time standard deviation drops to 00:39 (38th percentile).
*   Your midsleep point standard deviation is 00:57 (54th percentile), indicating moderate consistency in the midpoint of your sleep cycle.

### Sleep Quality
*   Your overall sleep score is 73.07 (12th percentile), which is quite low. This drops even further on workdays to 71.59 (9th percentile), though it recovers slightly on weekends to 76.23 (31st percentile).
*   Your REM sleep duration averages 01:09 (20th percentile) and deep sleep averages 01:06 (38th percentile), both of which are on the lower end. Conversely, your light sleep is slightly elevated at 04:47 (73rd percentile).
*   It takes you an average of 37 minutes to reach quality sleep, which is highly abnormal and prolonged (96th percentile).
*   Your wake time after sleep onset (WASO) is 14.69 minutes (83rd percentile), indicating frequent or prolonged awakenings during the night.
*   On a positive note, your restlessness score is 0.073 (26th percentile), meaning you remain relatively still while asleep.

### Alertness
*   You recorded 1 nap, but its duration was 164 minutes (99th percentile). A nap of nearly three hours is excessively long and likely interferes with your nighttime sleep drive and overall sleep quality.

### Timing
*   Your average bedtime is 22:11 (28th percentile), and your average wake time is 06:17 (28th percentile).
*   Interestingly, your weekend bedtime of 21:44 (11th percentile) is earlier than your workday bedtime of 22:23 (42nd percentile).
*   Despite going to bed earlier on weekends, your weekend wake time is significantly later at 07:55 (63rd percentile) compared to your early workday wake time of 05:31 (14th percentile).
*   This shift pushes your weekend midsleep point to 02:50 (32nd percentile), compared to an earlier 01:57 (24th percentile) on workdays.

### Efficiency
*   Your average sleep efficiency is 87.6% (43rd percentile), placing you slightly below the median compared to similar users.
*   Your wake time after sleep onset (14.69 minutes) is in the 83rd percentile, meaning you spend more time awake during the night than the vast majority of your peers (the top 95th percentile of similar users is 17.43 minutes).
*   Your time to quality sleep (37 minutes) is in the 96th percentile. You take longer to achieve restorative sleep than the top 95% of similar users, who reach it in 35 minutes or less.

### Duration
*   Your overall average sleep duration is 07:02, placing you exactly in the 50th percentile compared to similar users.
*   However, there is a drastic imbalance between your workday and weekend sleep. Your workday sleep duration is only 06:16 (19th percentile), while your weekend sleep duration jumps to 08:39 (94th percentile). You are likely using the weekends to recover from a significant workday sleep debt.
*   This massive discrepancy results in a sleep duration standard deviation of 01:52 (93rd percentile). Your day-to-day sleep totals fluctuate much more severely than most similar users.

Identify all of the patterns of data that are likely out of the preferred range. Make sure to consider various sleep health dimensions: Routine, Sleep Quality, Alertness, Timing, Efficiency, and Duration. Add a heading for each dimension. Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC24551.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
*   **High Workday Consistency:** Your wake time consistency on workdays is excellent, with a standard deviation of only 22 minutes (14th percentile). This indicates a strong alarm-based or biological habit during the week.
*   **Weekend Inconsistency:** In contrast, your weekend wake times vary significantly, with a standard deviation of 1 hour and 28 minutes (74th percentile). This lack of consistency between the week and weekend disrupts your circadian rhythm.

### Sleep Quality
*   **Low Deep Sleep:** Your average deep sleep is only 56 minutes, placing you in the 16th percentile. Deep sleep is crucial for physical restoration, and this amount is lower than optimal for your demographic.
*   **High Restlessness:** You have a high restlessness score of 0.115 (83rd percentile). This indicates you are physically moving or experiencing micro-awakenings more frequently than 83% of similar users, which fragments your sleep architecture.
*   **Healthy REM Duration:** Your REM sleep is robust, averaging 1 hour and 26 minutes (75th percentile), which supports cognitive processing and emotional regulation.

### Alertness
*   **No Napping:** You recorded zero naps (0th percentile). Since your workday sleep duration is low, monitor your daytime alertness levels carefully, as you are not using naps to compensate for sleep debt.

### Timing
*   **Social Jetlag:** There is a significant misalignment between your workday and weekend schedules. You wake up around 06:25 on workdays but sleep in until roughly 08:26 on weekends. This shifts your midsleep point by over an hour (02:44 on workdays vs. 03:47 on weekends), forcing your body to constantly readjust its internal clock.
*   **Consistent Bedtimes:** Unlike your wake times, your average bedtimes are very similar across the week (23:03 on workdays vs. 23:08 on weekends), suggesting your sleep onset routine is stable.

### Efficiency
*   **Low Sleep Efficiency:** Your average sleep efficiency is 85.7%, which is in the 14th percentile. This is considered low and suggests you spend a significant portion of your time in bed awake or in non-restorative stages.
*   **Delayed Quality Sleep:** It takes you an average of 28 minutes to reach quality sleep (83rd percentile). This latency contributes to your lower overall efficiency.

### Duration
*   **Workday Sleep Debt:** On workdays, you average only 6 hours and 19 minutes of sleep (35th percentile). This is likely insufficient for full restoration.
*   **Weekend Catch-up:** You exhibit a clear "catch-up" pattern, extending your sleep duration to 7 hours and 56 minutes on weekends (87th percentile). Relying on weekends to recover from weekday sleep loss is less effective than maintaining a consistent duration daily.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC26689.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly consistent. Your bedtime standard deviation is only 20 minutes, placing you in the 3rd percentile (indicating very little variation compared to peers). Your midsleep point standard deviation is also highly stable at 34 minutes (26th percentile), and your wake time standard deviation is 56 minutes (44th percentile). 

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep quality is strong, reflected by an average sleep score of 80.85 (72nd percentile). A standout positive pattern is your deep sleep duration, which averages 1 hour and 23 minutes, placing you exceptionally high in the 97th percentile. Your light sleep (73rd percentile) and REM sleep (62nd percentile) are also well within healthy ranges. However, your wake time after sleep onset is slightly elevated at 16.66 minutes (79th percentile).

### Alertness
You do not take any naps during the day, placing your nap frequency and average nap length of 0.0 minutes in the 0th percentile.

### Timing
You favor an early sleep schedule, with an average bedtime of 21:39 (16th percentile) and an early average midsleep point of 02:09 (27th percentile). However, there is a notable shift in your timing on weekends. While your workday wake time is 06:12 (36th percentile), your weekend wake time shifts nearly two hours later to 08:04 (80th percentile), indicating a pattern of weekend sleep extension or "social jetlag."

### Efficiency
Your average sleep efficiency is 86.3%, which is lower than preferred and places you in the 30th percentile compared to similar users. This reduced efficiency is likely driven by your higher-than-average wake time after sleep onset (79th percentile) and a slightly prolonged average time to quality sleep of 23 minutes (63rd percentile).

### Duration
You achieve an excellent amount of sleep, averaging 7 hours and 46 minutes overall (86th percentile). You sleep significantly longer on weekends, averaging 8 hours and 34 minutes (95th percentile), compared to 7 hours and 31 minutes on workdays (80th percentile). Despite the weekend extension, your day-to-day sleep duration remains highly consistent, with a standard deviation of just 42 minutes (13th percentile).

SC29698.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly irregular. Your bedtime standard deviation is 02:50 (95th percentile), and your wake time standard deviation is 03:24 (97th percentile). This severe inconsistency persists across both workdays (bedtime standard deviation in the 95th percentile, wake time standard deviation in the 96th percentile) and weekends (bedtime standard deviation in the 96th percentile, wake time standard deviation in the 96th percentile). Consequently, your midsleep point standard deviation is also highly variable at 03:01 (96th percentile).

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep score is 76.71 (46th percentile), which dips lower on weekends to 75.16 (33rd percentile). Your light sleep duration is notably low at 03:44 (13th percentile). However, your restorative sleep stages are strong: deep sleep averages 01:07 (74th percentile) and REM sleep averages 01:29 (90th percentile). Your restlessness score is average at 0.092 (55th percentile). You transition to quality sleep very quickly in just 10 minutes (8th percentile), and your wake time after sleep onset is low at 10.96 minutes (31st percentile).

### Alertness
You did not record any naps during this period, placing your total number of naps and average nap length in the 0th percentile. 

### Timing
Your sleep timing is significantly delayed compared to your peers. Your average bedtime is 02:22 (97th percentile) and your average wake time is 09:36 (97th percentile), resulting in a very late average midsleep point of 05:59 (97th percentile). Unusually, your sleep timing is slightly earlier on weekends (average bedtime 02:04, 96th percentile; wake time 08:53, 91st percentile) compared to workdays (average bedtime 02:29, 97th percentile; wake time 09:53, 97th percentile).

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is above average at 87.8% (65th percentile). This is driven by a very fast time to quality sleep of 10 minutes (8th percentile), which is near the bottom 5th percentile of similar users (9 minutes). Additionally, your wake time after sleep onset is minimal at 10.96 minutes (31st percentile), well below the top 95th percentile of 18.54 minutes for your demographic.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 06:20, which is below average (39th percentile) compared to similar users whose typical range is 05:15 to 07:45. Your sleep duration is particularly short on weekends, averaging just 05:55 (16th percentile), compared to an average of 06:30 on workdays (54th percentile). Furthermore, your sleep duration fluctuates significantly from day to day, with a standard deviation of 01:27 (78th percentile).

Identify all of the patterns of data that are likely out of the preferred range. Make sure to consider various sleep health dimensions: Routine, Sleep Quality, Alertness, Timing, Efficiency, and Duration. Add a heading for each dimension. Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. SC30662.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your sleep schedule shows significant inconsistency, particularly regarding when you wake up during the week. Your wake time standard deviation on workdays is in the **88th percentile** (1 hour 47 minutes), indicating highly irregular wake times compared to men your age. Similarly, your overall bedtime consistency varies more than average, with a standard deviation in the **61st percentile**. This "social jetlag" between days can disrupt your circadian rhythm.

### Sleep Quality
Despite spending enough time in bed, your overall Sleep Score is low, sitting in the **28th percentile** (average 75.07). This appears to be driven by a high proportion of Light Sleep (average 5 hours 19 minutes), which is in the **78th percentile**. While your Deep Sleep (41st percentile) and REM sleep (53rd percentile) are within normal ranges for your age group, the dominance of light sleep suggests your sleep architecture is somewhat fragile. Positively, your restlessness score is low (better than average) in the **23rd percentile**, meaning you are not physically tossing and turning significantly.

### Alertness
You recorded zero naps, placing you in the **0th percentile** for nap duration. This suggests you are maintaining wakefulness throughout the day without relying on daytime sleep to compensate for nighttime irregularities.

### Timing
Your average bedtime (22:27) and wake time (06:40) are generally typical, falling in the **38th** and **52nd percentiles** respectively. However, your data contains extreme outliers, such as a bedtime of 03:39 on the 9th day and 03:04 on the 29th day. These specific nights drastically shift your midsleep point and disrupt your biological clock.

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is healthy for your age group at 87.2%, placing you in the **60th percentile**. You fall asleep relatively quickly, with an average time to quality sleep of 15 minutes (**35th percentile**). Once asleep, you spend an average of 14 minutes awake (WASO), which is average (**53rd percentile**), indicating you do not struggle excessively with sleep maintenance insomnia.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration of 7 hours 10 minutes is good, ranking in the **69th percentile**. However, the stability of this duration is a concern. Your sleep duration standard deviation is in the **83rd percentile**, meaning your total sleep time fluctuates wildly from day to day (e.g., from 3 hours 4 minutes on the 29th day to 8 hours 44 minutes on the 8th day). While your average is healthy, the lack of consistency is likely impacting your overall recovery.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC31291.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your wake-up consistency on workdays requires attention. Your wake time standard deviation on workdays is 01:29, placing you in the 81st percentile, indicating your wake times fluctuate significantly more than similar users. While your bedtime consistency is average (52nd percentile), tightening your morning schedule could help regulate your circadian rhythm.

### Sleep Quality
You are achieving exceptional Deep Sleep. With an average of 01:19, you are in the 91st percentile, which is excellent for physical restoration and immune health. Additionally, your sleep is extremely stable; your restlessness score is in the 4th percentile, meaning you move significantly less than most peers. However, your Light Sleep is low (20th percentile), likely because your sleep architecture is prioritizing Deep Sleep.

### Alertness
You recorded zero naps during this period (0th percentile). This suggests you are maintaining wakefulness throughout the day without relying on daytime sleep to compensate for your nighttime duration.

### Timing
You tend to be an early riser compared to your demographic. Your average wake time of 05:49 falls in the 26th percentile. Your average bedtime of 22:19 is in the 40th percentile. There is a slight shift in your schedule on weekends, where you wake up approximately 41 minutes later (06:18) than on workdays.

### Efficiency
Your overall sleep efficiency is healthy at 88.1% (69th percentile). However, your time to quality sleep (sleep latency) averages 26 minutes, placing you in the 74th percentile. This indicates it takes you longer to settle into stable sleep than the majority of similar users, suggesting potential difficulties with sleep onset or pre-sleep relaxation.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration of 06:37 is somewhat short, placing you in the 35th percentile. While this may be sufficient for some, it is on the lower end of the spectrum. Notably, there is significant inconsistency in your duration (standard deviation in the 59th percentile), highlighted by a severe outlier on the 28th day where you only slept 02:36. Eliminating such drastic drops in duration is crucial for long-term health.

Make sure to consider various sleep health dimensions: Routine, Sleep Quality, Alertness, Timing, Efficiency, and Duration. Add a heading for each dimension. Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC31804.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your sleep schedule exhibits extreme inconsistency. Your bedtime standard deviation is 2 hours and 23 minutes, placing you in the 94th percentile for irregularity. Similarly, your wake time standard deviation is 2 hours and 7 minutes (90th percentile). This high degree of variability suggests you lack a stable circadian rhythm, which can significantly impact your body's ability to anticipate sleep.

### Sleep Quality
Your sleep architecture is concerning, particularly regarding REM sleep. Your average REM duration is only 28 minutes, placing you in the 2nd percentile, which is critically low and may impact cognitive restoration. Deep sleep is also reduced at an average of 35 minutes (16th percentile). Consequently, your overall average sleep score is 74.2 (24th percentile). On a positive note, your restlessness score is low (25th percentile), indicating that you are not physically agitated during the night.

### Alertness
You recorded 5 naps with an average duration of 98.6 minutes. This nap length is in the 91st percentile. Naps of this duration constitute full sleep cycles rather than restorative "power naps," suggesting you are using daytime sleep to compensate for insufficient nighttime duration.

### Timing
Your sleep timing is significantly delayed compared to your peers. Your average bedtime is 00:22 (87th percentile), and your average midsleep point is 03:44 (80th percentile). There is a notable disparity between your workday and weekend timing; your average workday bedtime is very late at 00:44 (90th percentile), likely driven by extreme outliers in your logs (e.g., bedtimes around 07:50 AM on Tuesdays), whereas your weekend bedtime is earlier at 23:22 (63rd percentile).

### Efficiency
Despite the irregularity of your schedule, your ability to maintain sleep once initiated is average. Your sleep efficiency is 86.9% (53rd percentile), and your average wake time after sleep onset is 14.2 minutes (51st percentile). This indicates that your primary issue is not sleep fragmentation, but rather sleep opportunity and structure.

### Duration
You are chronically sleep-deprived. Your average sleep duration is 5 hours and 50 minutes, which falls in the 16th percentile. This is even more pronounced on weekends, where your average duration drops to 5 hours and 31 minutes (8th percentile). Given your age group, this duration is likely insufficient for optimal health and recovery.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC36080.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your bedtime consistency on workdays is excellent; with a standard deviation of only 39 minutes, you are more consistent than 73% of similar users (27th percentile). However, your wake times on workdays are highly variable (standard deviation of 1 hour 21 minutes, 76th percentile). Interestingly, your weekend wake time is extremely rigid with a standard deviation of only 22 minutes (5th percentile), indicating you wake up at nearly the exact same time every Saturday and Sunday, unlike your workdays.

### Sleep Quality
Your sleep architecture shows an imbalance. Your average deep sleep is only 49 minutes, placing you in the bottom 20th percentile. Conversely, you spend a significant amount of time in light sleep (5 hours 1 minute), which is in the 88th percentile. While your restlessness score is low (27th percentile), you spend more time awake after initially falling asleep (14.6 minutes) than 80% of your peers, suggesting some sleep fragmentation.

### Alertness
You rarely rely on daytime sleep, recording only one nap over the logged period. However, that single nap was 70 minutes long, which is substantial and may have impacted your sleep pressure that specific night.

### Timing
You exhibit a "night owl" tendency. Your average bedtime (23:53) and wake time (07:53) are in the 79th and 89th percentiles, respectively, meaning you sleep and wake significantly later than most men your age. You also experience "social jetlag": your body clock shifts by over an hour on weekends (midsleep 04:45) compared to workdays (midsleep 03:30).

### Efficiency
Your overall sleep efficiency is healthy at 88.1% (64th percentile). You fall asleep relatively quickly (16 minutes, 35th percentile). However, your Wake After Sleep Onset (WASO) is in the 80th percentile, meaning once you are asleep, you spend more time awake during the night than the average user in your demographic.

### Duration
You are getting a sufficient quantity of sleep. Your average duration of 7 hours and 2 minutes places you in the 75th percentile. This is consistent across the week, with your workday duration (6 hours 59 minutes) ranking in the 78th percentile compared to similar users.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. SC37359.insights_output:
### Routine
Your wake time on workdays is highly consistent, with a standard deviation of just 31 minutes (20th percentile). However, your weekend wake time variability is much higher, with a standard deviation of 1 hour and 24 minutes (70th percentile). Your overall bedtime standard deviation is moderate at 1 hour and 2 minutes (53rd percentile), and your midsleep point standard deviation is 54 minutes (42nd percentile). 

### Sleep Quality
You spend a disproportionately large amount of time in light sleep, averaging 4 hours and 48 minutes (81st percentile). Consequently, your restorative sleep stages are slightly below average, with deep sleep at 1 hour and 20 minutes (38th percentile) and REM sleep at 1 hour and 12 minutes (31st percentile). It also takes you a prolonged 30 minutes to reach quality sleep (92nd percentile). Despite this, your overall sleep score is strong at 80.66 (71st percentile), your wake time after sleep onset is very low at 9.05 minutes (18th percentile), and your restlessness score is average at 0.095 (54th percentile).

### Alertness
You rarely nap, recording only 1 nap during this 28-day period. However, that single nap lasted 56 minutes, placing it in the 70th percentile for length. 

### Timing
You maintain an early sleep schedule. Your average bedtime is 22:36 (27th percentile), and your average wake time is 06:54 (32nd percentile), resulting in an early average midsleep point of 02:45 (27th percentile). You shift your schedule noticeably on weekends, going to bed over an hour later at 23:26 (37th percentile) and waking up over an hour later at 07:46 (32nd percentile) compared to your workday averages.

### Efficiency
Your overall sleep efficiency is excellent at 88.9%, placing you in the 83rd percentile compared to similar users. This is largely driven by your minimal wake time after sleep onset (18th percentile). However, your time to quality sleep is a significant outlier; at 30 minutes, it sits in the 92nd percentile, meaning it takes you much longer than similar users to transition into deeper, restorative sleep stages.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is a healthy 7 hours and 22 minutes (69th percentile compared to similar users). You maintain this duration consistently across the week, with workday sleep duration at 7 hours and 22 minutes (75th percentile) and weekend duration at 7 hours and 23 minutes (47th percentile). Your overall sleep duration standard deviation is very low at 49 minutes (28th percentile), indicating highly stable sleep lengths compared to your peers.

Identify all of the patterns of data that are likely out of the preferred range. Make sure to consider various sleep health dimensions: Routine, Sleep Quality, Alertness, Timing, Efficiency, and Duration. Add a heading for each dimension. Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC37892.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly consistent. Your overall bedtime standard deviation is 53 minutes (36th percentile), but on workdays, it is exceptionally stable with a standard deviation of just 15 minutes (1st percentile). Your wake time is also very consistent, with a standard deviation of 40 minutes (16th percentile). Your midsleep point standard deviation is 41 minutes (28th percentile), further reflecting a strong, stable routine.

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep quality is a primary area of concern. Your average sleep score is 74.04 (17th percentile), which drops significantly to 70.97 (6th percentile) on weekends. This low score is heavily driven by a very high restlessness score of 0.131 (94th percentile). Additionally, your sleep architecture is imbalanced: you spend an unusually high amount of time in light sleep (5 hours and 43 minutes, 97th percentile) and very little time in REM sleep (1 hour and 5 minutes, 14th percentile). Your deep sleep duration is slightly below average at 1 hour and 7 minutes (41st percentile). Your time to quality sleep is normal at 20 minutes (48th percentile). 

### Alertness
You recorded 0 naps with an average nap length of 0 minutes (0th percentile). This suggests you are able to maintain adequate daytime alertness without relying on supplemental daytime sleep.

### Timing
You exhibit a noticeable shift in your sleep timing between workdays and weekends. On workdays, your average bedtime is 22:19 (39th percentile) and your wake time is 07:39 (86th percentile). On weekends, your bedtime shifts significantly later to 00:02 (73rd percentile), and your wake time shifts to 08:45 (82nd percentile). Consequently, your midsleep point shifts from 02:59 on workdays (68th percentile) to a much later 04:23 on weekends (80th percentile).

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is 86.5%, which is low and places you in the 19th percentile compared to similar users. While your time to quality sleep is average (20 minutes, 48th percentile), your wake time after sleep onset is elevated at 15.07 minutes (85th percentile). This indicates that once you fall asleep, you spend more time awake during the night than the vast majority of your peers.

### Duration
You achieve an excellent and highly stable amount of sleep compared to similar users. Your average sleep duration is 7 hours and 55 minutes (91st percentile). Interestingly, you sleep significantly longer on workdays (8 hours and 5 minutes, 96th percentile) than you do on weekends (7 hours and 27 minutes, 56th percentile). Your overall sleep duration standard deviation is just 39 minutes (11th percentile), showing great night-to-night consistency in the total amount of rest you get.

SC39156.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your sleep schedule shows significant inconsistency, particularly regarding wake times. Your wake time standard deviation is in the 91st percentile, indicating highly irregular mornings. This is even more pronounced on weekends, where your wake time variability spikes to the 96th percentile. Similarly, your bedtime consistency is poor, with a standard deviation in the 79th percentile overall and the 91st percentile on weekends. This high variability suggests a lack of a stable circadian rhythm.

### Sleep Quality
Despite the irregular schedule, your physiological sleep quality is quite strong. Your average sleep score is in the 73rd percentile (80.53). Notably, your restlessness score is very low (16th percentile), meaning you move very little during sleep compared to men your age. Your Deep sleep (56th percentile) and REM sleep (42nd percentile) durations are within normal ranges, suggesting that when you do sleep, your brain cycles through sleep stages effectively.

### Alertness
You recorded zero naps during this period. Given the irregularity of your sleep duration, monitoring daytime fatigue is important, though the lack of naps suggests you are maintaining daytime wakefulness.

### Timing
Your average bedtime is 22:52 (59th percentile) and average wake time is 06:14 (46th percentile). However, these averages hide extreme outliers. For example, you have bedtimes as late as 04:17 and 03:02, contrasting sharply with your earliest bedtime of 21:14. Your midsleep point varies significantly (standard deviation in the 87th percentile), confirming that your internal body clock is being shifted constantly.

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is healthy at 86.6% (41st percentile). Your time to quality sleep (sleep latency) averages 19 minutes (61st percentile), and your wake time after sleep onset averages roughly 13.5 minutes (54th percentile). These metrics indicate that you do not struggle significantly with falling asleep or staying asleep once in bed, which is excellent for the 60-64 age group.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 06:22, placing you in the 36th percentile, which is on the lower end of the preferred range. A major concern is your weekend sleep duration, which averages only 05:54 (13th percentile). Your sleep duration standard deviation is in the 89th percentile, meaning your total sleep time fluctuates wildly from day to day (ranging from a low of 02:28 to a high of 08:43). This inconsistency prevents you from establishing a restorative baseline.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC41248.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly consistent. Your overall bedtime variation is low (standard deviation of 48 minutes, 24th percentile), indicating a stable nightly routine. Notably, your wake times on the weekend are exceptionally consistent (standard deviation of 27 minutes, 9th percentile), which helps maintain a strong circadian rhythm.

### Sleep Quality
While your overall Sleep Score is healthy (average 80.7, 74th percentile), there is a significant issue with sleep onset or stabilization. Your average "time to quality sleep" is 1 hour and 9 minutes, placing you in the 99th percentile. This suggests you struggle to settle into stable sleep initially. Once asleep, your sleep architecture is solid, with a high amount of Light Sleep (4 hours 57 minutes, 82nd percentile) and normal ranges for Deep and REM sleep.

### Alertness
You do not record any naps (0th percentile). This suggests that despite efficiency issues, your main sleep period is sufficient to sustain you throughout the day without requiring daytime recovery sleep.

### Timing
You tend to go to bed earlier than your peers, with an average bedtime of 22:12 (25th percentile). Your "social jetlag" (the shift between workday and weekend timing) is minimal; your average weekend bedtime (22:30) is only slightly later than your workday bedtime (22:05), which is excellent for sleep hygiene.

### Efficiency
This is your primary area of concern. Your sleep efficiency is 84.8%, which is in the 3rd percentile (very low compared to similar users). This is driven by a high Wake After Sleep Onset (WASO) average of ~22 minutes (99th percentile) and the previously mentioned long time to reach quality sleep. You spend a significant amount of time in bed awake or in very light, fragmented sleep compared to your peers.

### Duration
You are getting a sufficient quantity of sleep. Your average sleep duration is 7 hours and 48 minutes, placing you in the 87th percentile. You maintain this duration well across the week, with workdays averaging 7 hours 40 minutes (84th percentile) and weekends averaging 8 hours 8 minutes (83rd percentile). Your duration consistency is excellent (standard deviation of 41 minutes, 11th percentile).
