Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. SC41758.insights_output:
### Routine
*   **Highly Irregular Schedule:** Your sleep routine is highly inconsistent. Your bedtime standard deviation of 02:35 places you in the 86th percentile for variability. 
*   **Workday Wake Time Volatility:** Your wake time standard deviation is 03:15 (86th percentile), but on workdays, this variability spikes to 03:43, placing you in the 92nd percentile.
*   **Midsleep Inconsistency:** Your midsleep point standard deviation is 02:48, placing you in the 87th percentile, further indicating a lack of a stable day-to-day sleep routine.

### Sleep Quality
*   **Low Light and Deep Sleep:** Your light sleep duration averages 03:54 (20th percentile) and your deep sleep averages 01:10 (29th percentile), both of which are on the lower end. 
*   **Normal REM and Restlessness:** Your REM sleep is healthy at 01:28 (58th percentile), and your restlessness score is low at 0.077 (25th percentile), which is a positive indicator of undisturbed sleep.
*   **Average Overall Score:** Your average sleep score is 78.79 (55th percentile), reflecting moderate overall sleep quality despite the lack of deep sleep.
*   **Delayed Quality Sleep:** It takes you an average of 27 minutes to reach quality sleep, placing you in the 78th percentile (meaning it takes you longer than most). 

### Alertness
*   **Long Naps:** You took 4 naps during this period. Your average nap length is 75.75 minutes (60th percentile). Naps of this length can induce sleep inertia (grogginess) and potentially interfere with your nighttime sleep drive.

### Timing
*   **Early Sleep Schedule:** Your overall sleep timing leans earlier than average. Your average wake time is 06:51 (25th percentile), your average bedtime is 23:18 (43rd percentile), and your average midsleep point is 03:05 (34th percentile).
*   **Weekend Shift:** You exhibit a noticeable shift in your weekend timing. Your average weekend bedtime (23:58, 47th percentile) is nearly an hour later than your workday bedtime (23:01, 43rd percentile), and your weekend wake time (07:42, 29th percentile) is over an hour later than your workday wake time (06:30, 26th percentile).

### Efficiency
*   **Below Average Efficiency:** Your average sleep efficiency is 87% (33rd percentile). This sits closer to the bottom 5% of similar users (85%) than the top 95% (90%).
*   **Prolonged Time to Quality Sleep:** Your time to quality sleep (27 minutes, 78th percentile) is significantly higher than the bottom 5% of similar users (12 minutes) and approaches the top 95% (36 minutes).
*   **Low Wake After Sleep Onset (WASO):** Once asleep, you stay asleep well. Your WASO is only 10.43 minutes (34th percentile), which is very close to the bottom 5% of similar users (7.40 minutes) and well below the top 95% (16.72 minutes).

### Duration
*   **Short Overall Duration:** Your average sleep duration is 06:34, placing you in the 20th percentile. This is much closer to the bottom 5% of similar users (05:52) than the top 95% (08:08).
*   **Workday vs. Weekend:** You sleep even less on workdays, averaging 06:26 (22nd percentile). You attempt to catch up on weekends, averaging 06:50 (27th percentile).
*   **High Duration Variability:** Your sleep duration fluctuates significantly from day to day, with a standard deviation of 01:32, placing you in the 71st percentile for duration variability.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC44077.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly consistent. Your bedtime standard deviation is just 37 minutes (12th percentile), indicating very little day-to-day fluctuation. Your midsleep point standard deviation is 45 minutes (33rd percentile), and your wake time standard deviation is 1 hour and 7 minutes (48th percentile), showing a stable overall routine.

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep quality is below average, reflected by an average sleep score of 73.90 (21st percentile), which drops even further to 72.96 (18th percentile) on workdays. Your sleep architecture shows reduced durations across all stages: light sleep averages 3 hours and 49 minutes (21st percentile), deep sleep is 55 minutes (27th percentile), and REM sleep is 1 hour and 6 minutes (37th percentile). You take 21 minutes to reach quality sleep (61st percentile) and spend 12.27 minutes awake after sleep onset (57th percentile). On a positive note, your restlessness score is low at 0.071 (24th percentile), indicating you sleep relatively still.

### Alertness
You rarely nap, recording only 1 nap during this period. However, the duration of this nap was exceptionally long at 115 minutes (93rd percentile). Naps of this length can significantly interfere with your nighttime sleep drive.

### Timing
Your sleep timing is significantly delayed. Your average bedtime is 01:04 (94th percentile) and your average wake time is 07:50 (87th percentile), resulting in a late average midsleep point of 04:27 (92nd percentile). This late-shift pattern is even more pronounced on weekends, where your average bedtime pushes to 01:18 (91st percentile) and your wake time extends to 09:06 (91st percentile). 

### Efficiency
Your average sleep efficiency is 86.8%, which is below average (37th percentile) compared to similar users whose typical range is between 84.0% and 90.1%. This reduced efficiency is influenced by taking 21 minutes to reach quality sleep (61st percentile) and a wake time after sleep onset of 12.27 minutes (57th percentile), both of which are slightly higher than average compared to your peers.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration of 5 hours and 51 minutes is critically short, placing you in the 15th percentile compared to similar users (who typically sleep between 5 hours 23 minutes and 7 hours 44 minutes). This deficit is severe on workdays, where you average only 5 hours and 30 minutes (10th percentile). You attempt to recover on weekends, increasing your sleep to 6 hours and 44 minutes (45th percentile). Your overall sleep duration standard deviation is 52 minutes (32nd percentile).

Identify all of the patterns of data that are likely out of the preferred range. Make sure to consider various sleep health dimensions: Routine, Sleep Quality, Alertness, Timing, Efficiency, and Duration. Add a heading for each dimension. Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC44447.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your consistency during the workweek is exceptional. Your workday bedtime standard deviation is in the 4th percentile (±18 minutes) and your workday wake time standard deviation is in the 2nd percentile (±15 minutes), indicating a very rigid schedule Monday through Friday. However, this consistency drops significantly on the weekend, where your wake time variability jumps to the 49th percentile.

### Sleep Quality
Your sleep architecture shows a unique pattern. You are achieving excellent Deep Sleep duration, ranking in the 86th percentile (average 01:38), which is vital for physical restoration. However, your Light Sleep duration is extremely low, falling into the 7th percentile (average 03:35). Additionally, your sleep is somewhat fragmented; your restlessness score is in the 71st percentile, indicating you move or are disturbed more often than average. Consequently, your overall Sleep Score on workdays is low (29th percentile), whereas your weekend Sleep Score is much healthier (77th percentile).

### Alertness
You recorded 4 naps with an average length of 91.5 minutes. This duration is in the 77th percentile and constitutes a full sleep cycle. While napping can be beneficial, naps of this length strongly suggest you are compensating for significant accumulated sleep debt from your workdays.

### Timing
You are experiencing "Social Jetlag"—a misalignment between your biological clock and social schedule. There is a drastic shift between your average workday wake time (06:20) and your weekend wake time (08:48). This shift pushes your weekend midsleep point (04:32) nearly two hours later than your workday midsleep point (02:45). This constant shifting can strain your circadian rhythm.

### Efficiency
Your average sleep efficiency is 86.8%, which places you in the 23rd percentile. While your time to fall asleep is average (50th percentile) and your time awake after sleep onset is actually quite good (33rd percentile), the overall ratio of time spent in bed versus time asleep is lower than preferred for your demographic.

### Duration
Your total sleep duration is the most concerning metric. Your average workday sleep duration is only 6 hours and 11 minutes, placing you in the 13th percentile. This is significantly below the recommended range for an adult female under 30. You are attempting to recover this lost time on weekends, where your duration increases to 7 hours and 25 minutes (41st percentile), but this "catch-up" sleep is insufficient to address the chronic deficit accumulated during the week.

SC44948.insights_output:
### Routine
*   Your wake time is highly variable, with an overall standard deviation of 01:27 (75th percentile). This inconsistency persists across both workdays (01:26 standard deviation, 79th percentile) and weekends (01:33 standard deviation, 80th percentile).
*   Your bedtime is moderately consistent overall (01:00 standard deviation, 51st percentile), but it is highly consistent on weekends (00:18 standard deviation, 7th percentile) compared to workdays (01:02 standard deviation, 58th percentile).
*   Your midsleep point standard deviation is 00:57 (65th percentile), reflecting the shifting of your sleep window.

### Sleep Quality
*   Your deep sleep duration is critically low at just 00:26, placing you in the 3rd percentile. This falls below the bottom 5th percentile threshold (00:28) for similar users.
*   Your light sleep duration is elevated at 05:14 (72nd percentile), while your REM sleep is slightly below average at 00:54 (34th percentile).
*   Your overall sleep score is low at 72.67 (15th percentile). This drops even further on workdays to 71.85 (12th percentile).
*   Your wake time after sleep onset is exceptionally high at 20.92 minutes (95th percentile), indicating significant sleep fragmentation.
*   On a positive note, your restlessness score is low at 0.071 (22nd percentile).

### Alertness
*   You recorded 3 naps during this period.
*   Your average nap length is 29.67 minutes (47th percentile), which is an optimal duration for a quick recovery without causing sleep inertia.

### Timing
*   You maintain an early sleep schedule. Your average bedtime is 21:45 (18th percentile), and your average wake time is 05:35 (19th percentile).
*   Your weekend bedtime is notably earlier at 21:02 (6th percentile) compared to your workday bedtime of 22:01 (27th percentile).
*   Your average midsleep point is early at 01:40 (17th percentile), shifting even earlier on weekends to 01:24 (10th percentile).

### Efficiency
*   Your overall sleep efficiency is low at 84.6% (13th percentile), sitting very close to the bottom 5th percentile of similar users (83.5%).
*   This reduced efficiency is heavily driven by your wake time after sleep onset (20.92 minutes, 95th percentile), which exceeds the top 95th percentile threshold (20.66 minutes) for your demographic.
*   Conversely, your time to quality sleep is very fast at just 11 minutes (12th percentile), meaning you transition into quality sleep quickly despite waking up frequently later in the night.

### Duration
*   Your average sleep duration is 06:35 (42nd percentile), which is typical for your demographic.
*   You sleep significantly longer on weekends, averaging 07:25 (72nd percentile), compared to just 06:17 on workdays (34th percentile).
*   Your sleep duration standard deviation is 01:15 (65th percentile), indicating moderate-to-high day-to-day variability in your total sleep time.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC47624.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule shows significant variability, particularly on your days off. Your overall bedtime standard deviation is 01:34 (72nd percentile), and your wake time standard deviation is 01:45 (78th percentile). While your workday wake times are relatively consistent with a standard deviation of just 00:43 (39th percentile), your weekend routine fluctuates heavily, showing a bedtime standard deviation of 02:04 (83rd percentile) and a wake time standard deviation of 02:48 (91st percentile). Your overall midsleep point standard deviation is also high at 01:30 (79th percentile).

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep score is a healthy 80.03 (71st percentile), though it drops notably on weekends to 77.94 (47th percentile) compared to 81.02 (79th percentile) on workdays. Your light sleep duration is 04:22 (52nd percentile) and REM sleep is 01:10 (48th percentile), both of which are typical. However, your deep sleep duration is on the lower end at 00:53 (30th percentile). It also takes you an average of 00:25 to reach quality sleep (78th percentile), and your wake time after sleep onset is slightly elevated at 13.55 minutes (68th percentile). Your restlessness score remains normal at 0.091 (54th percentile).

### Alertness
You recorded 0 naps with an average nap length of 0.0 minutes (0th percentile). Avoiding daytime napping is generally a strong habit for building sufficient sleep drive for the night.

### Timing
Your sleep timing is shifted significantly later than most men your age. Your average bedtime is 00:22 (86th percentile), your average wake time is 07:54 (89th percentile), and your average midsleep point is 04:08 (89th percentile). This late-shifting pattern is heavily exaggerated on weekends, where your average bedtime gets pushed to 01:13 (91st percentile) and your wake time extends to 08:47 (88th percentile), compared to a workday bedtime of 23:58 (83rd percentile) and wake time of 07:29 (87th percentile). 

### Efficiency
Your average sleep efficiency is 85.8%, which is quite low and places you in the 20th percentile compared to similar users. This reduced efficiency is directly impacted by your elevated time to quality sleep (00:25, 78th percentile) and your wake time after sleep onset (13.55 minutes, 68th percentile), indicating that you spend a larger-than-average portion of your time in bed awake or in very light, non-restorative stages before settling into deeper sleep.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 06:27, which is slightly below average (43rd percentile compared to similar users). Interestingly, you do not use weekends to catch up on sleep; your weekend sleep duration is 06:29 (31st percentile), which is nearly identical to your workday sleep duration of 06:25 (50th percentile). Your overall sleep duration standard deviation is 01:13 (60th percentile), showing moderate day-to-day fluctuation in the total hours you sleep.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. SC47817.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule is exceptionally consistent. Your overall bedtime standard deviation is just 33 minutes (9th percentile), and your wake time standard deviation is 44 minutes (17th percentile). This routine is even more rigid on workdays, where your bedtime standard deviation drops to 14 minutes (1st percentile) and your wake time standard deviation is a mere 9 minutes (2nd percentile). Your midsleep point standard deviation is also highly stable at 35 minutes (15th percentile).

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep quality is solid, reflected by an average sleep score of 79.93 (65th percentile). You experience remarkably low physical disturbance, with a restlessness score of 0.045 (1st percentile) and an extremely low wake time after sleep onset of 6.7 minutes (1st percentile). Your deep sleep duration is excellent at 1 hour and 15 minutes (81st percentile), while your REM sleep is average at 1 hour and 22 minutes (55th percentile). However, your light sleep duration is notably low at 3 hours and 34 minutes (7th percentile). Your time to quality sleep is typical at 21 minutes (56th percentile).

### Alertness
You show strong signs of daytime alertness, having recorded only 1 nap over the entire tracking period. This single nap lasted 59 minutes, which falls in the 55th percentile for nap length. 

### Timing
You have a significantly advanced sleep phase, meaning you sleep and wake much earlier than your peers. Your average bedtime is 21:10 (7th percentile), and your average wake time is 04:06 (1st percentile). This results in an exceptionally early average midsleep point of 00:38 (2nd percentile). You do allow yourself to sleep in slightly on weekends, shifting your average wake time to 05:07 and bedtime to 21:45, compared to a very early 03:40 wake time and 20:55 bedtime on workdays.

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is highly optimal at 89.7% (87th percentile), sitting comfortably above the bottom 5th percentile of similar users (84.7%). This high efficiency is largely driven by your ability to stay asleep; your wake time after sleep onset (6.7 minutes, 1st percentile) is exceptionally lower than the top 95th percentile of your peers (18.4 minutes). Your time to quality sleep (21 minutes, 56th percentile) is perfectly average compared to similar users.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 6 hours and 12 minutes, which is short and places you in the 18th percentile compared to similar users. You get slightly more rest on weekends, averaging 6 hours and 41 minutes (28th percentile), compared to exactly 6 hours on workdays (17th percentile). While your total sleep time is below the generally recommended 7 hours, your day-to-day sleep duration is incredibly consistent, with a standard deviation of only 32 minutes (3rd percentile).

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. SC51543.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your sleep schedule is exceptionally consistent compared to women your age. Your bedtime standard deviation is only 40 minutes, placing you in the 18th percentile, indicating you stick to a very regular nightly schedule. Similarly, your midsleep point variability is low (22nd percentile), suggesting your internal body clock is well-stabilized.

### Sleep Quality
Your sleep architecture and overall quality are outstanding. You achieve an average sleep score of 84.79, placing you in the top 4% (96th percentile) of similar users. Your restorative sleep stages are particularly high: you average 1 hour 19 minutes of Deep Sleep (88th percentile) and 1 hour 42 minutes of REM Sleep (92nd percentile). Consequently, your Light Sleep is lower (7th percentile), but this is a positive trade-off for higher quality stages. Furthermore, your sleep is incredibly peaceful; your restlessness score is in the 1st percentile, meaning you move significantly less than almost all peers.

### Alertness
You recorded zero naps during this period. This suggests you are likely maintaining sufficient alertness throughout the day without requiring supplemental rest.

### Timing
Your sleep timing is socially aligned and consistent between workdays and weekends. Your average bedtime is 22:57 (62nd percentile) and wake time is 06:28 (47th percentile). There is minimal "social jetlag" in your data; your weekend bedtime (23:01) and wake time (06:47) shift only slightly compared to your workdays, which supports your strong circadian rhythm.

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is high at 89.3% (82nd percentile), indicating you spend the vast majority of your time in bed actually asleep. This is supported by a low Wake After Sleep Onset (WASO) average of just over 9 minutes (16th percentile), showing you rarely struggle to stay asleep once you drift off. Your time to quality sleep averages 24 minutes (73rd percentile), which is within a normal, healthy latency range.

### Duration
While your quality is high, your total sleep duration is slightly below the average for your demographic. You average 6 hours and 42 minutes per night, placing you in the 40th percentile. While this may be sufficient given your high sleep efficiency, there are specific nights where duration drops significantly (e.g., 4 hours 10 minutes on Day 9), which drags down your weekly averages.

SC53368.insights_output:
# Sleep Insights Report

### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly consistent. Your overall bedtime standard deviation is 41 minutes (22nd percentile), and your midsleep point standard deviation is 33 minutes (24th percentile). Your workday bedtime routine is exceptionally strict, with a standard deviation of just 27 minutes (10th percentile). However, your bedtime fluctuates slightly more on weekends, showing a standard deviation of 1 hour and 6 minutes (57th percentile). Your wake time standard deviation is 51 minutes (38th percentile), indicating a stable morning routine.

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep quality shows some imbalances that are out of the preferred range. Your average sleep score is 76.86 (36th percentile), which is below average. Your sleep architecture is heavily skewed toward light sleep, averaging 5 hours and 42 minutes (92nd percentile). Consequently, your REM sleep is notably deficient at just 52 minutes (13th percentile), and your deep sleep is slightly below average at 49 minutes (33rd percentile). Additionally, it takes you an average of 33 minutes to reach quality sleep, which is unusually long (90th percentile). On a positive note, your sleep is physically calm; your average restlessness score is very low at 0.060 (14th percentile).

### Alertness
You did not take any naps during this logging period, placing your average nap length and frequency in the 0th percentile. 

### Timing
You maintain an early sleep schedule compared to similar users. Your average bedtime is 21:58 (23rd percentile), and your average wake time is 06:31 (42nd percentile), resulting in an early average midsleep point of 02:15 (31st percentile). Your timing shifts slightly depending on the day of the week: while your weekend bedtime (21:59) is nearly identical to your workday bedtime (21:57), you allow yourself to sleep in later on weekends, waking up at 06:56 compared to 06:20 on workdays.

### Efficiency
Your average sleep efficiency is 86.5% (32nd percentile), which is on the lower end when compared to similar users (whose typical efficiency ranges from 84.3% to 90.0%). Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC53881.insights_output:
### Routine
Your bedtime is highly consistent, with a standard deviation of just 52 minutes (22nd percentile). However, your wake time shows an out-of-range pattern of high variability, particularly on workdays where the standard deviation spikes to 2 hours and 5 minutes (83rd percentile). Your midsleep point standard deviation remains relatively stable at 58 minutes (40th percentile). 

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep quality is strong, reflected by an average sleep score of 80.56 (73rd percentile). You get an excellent amount of REM sleep, averaging 1 hour and 43 minutes (88th percentile), and solid deep sleep at 1 hour and 20 minutes (63rd percentile). Your light sleep averages 4 hours and 12 minutes (33rd percentile). You transition to quality sleep quickly (17 minutes, 30th percentile) and experience minimal disruptions, with a wake time after sleep onset of just 9.56 minutes (22nd percentile). Your restlessness score is normal at 0.085 (47th percentile).

### Alertness
You do not take any daytime naps, placing your total number of naps and average nap length in the 0th percentile compared to similar users. 

### Timing
Your sleep timing is relatively standard but shifts slightly later on weekends. Your average bedtime is 22:56 (42nd percentile), moving from 22:53 on workdays (49th percentile) to 23:05 on weekends (33rd percentile). Your average wake time is 07:06 (42nd percentile), shifting from 06:58 on workdays (52nd percentile) to 07:29 on weekends (30th percentile). Consequently, your midsleep point shifts from 02:55 on workdays (50th percentile) to 03:17 on weekends (29th percentile).

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is excellent at 89.3%, placing you in the 84th percentile compared to similar users (whose top 95th percentile is 90.0%). This high efficiency is driven by a very low average wake time after sleep onset of 9.56 minutes (22nd percentile, well below the 16.57-minute top 95th percentile of your peers) and a quick average time to quality sleep of 17 minutes (30th percentile).

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 7 hours and 15 minutes (56th percentile), which sits comfortably within the normal range of similar users (bottom 5th is 5 hours 55 minutes, top 95th is 8 hours 8 minutes). You sleep slightly longer on weekends (7 hours 25 minutes, 48th percentile) compared to workdays (7 hours 12 minutes, 59th percentile). However, your sleep duration standard deviation is 1 hour and 39 minutes (82nd percentile). This is out of the preferred range and indicates significant day-to-day fluctuations in your total sleep time, largely driven by an extreme 14-hour sleep event on your 9th logged day.

SC57352.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly inconsistent. Your bedtime standard deviation is 03:34 (94th percentile), and your wake time standard deviation is 05:35 (97th percentile). This severe irregularity persists throughout the entire week, with a workday bedtime standard deviation of 03:28 (94th percentile) and a weekend wake time standard deviation of 05:41 (96th percentile). Consequently, your midsleep point standard deviation is also extremely high at 04:26 (97th percentile). 

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep score is 71.85, placing you in the bottom 10th percentile, and it drops even lower on workdays to 69.95 (5th percentile). Your sleep architecture shows lower-than-average durations across all stages: light sleep averages 03:50 (21st percentile), deep sleep is 01:08 (32nd percentile), and REM sleep is 01:10 (34th percentile). You experience high restlessness, with an average score of 0.133 (88th percentile). Additionally, your time to quality sleep is prolonged at 00:33 (92nd percentile), though your wake time after sleep onset remains relatively low at 10.08 (34th percentile).

### Alertness
You recorded 4 naps during this period. Your average nap length is exceptionally long at 129.25 minutes, placing you in the 92nd percentile. 

### Timing
There is a drastic shift in your sleep timing between workdays and weekends. Your average workday bedtime is 23:58 (60th percentile) with a wake time of 06:24 (27th percentile). On weekends, your timing shifts significantly later: your average bedtime is 01:35 (72nd percentile) and your wake time is 10:26 (84th percentile). This creates a large delay in your midsleep point, moving from 03:11 on workdays (45th percentile) to 06:01 on weekends (81st percentile).

### Efficiency
Your average sleep efficiency is 0.861 (26th percentile), which is on the lower end compared to similar users whose bottom 5th percentile is 0.842 and top 95th is 0.897. While your wake time after sleep onset of 10.08 (34th percentile) is well within the normal range of similar users (7.40 to 16.89), your time to quality sleep is 00:33 (92nd percentile). This is near the top 95th percentile (00:35) of similar users, indicating you take much longer than average to settle into restorative sleep.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 06:09 (18th percentile), which is significantly shorter than the top 95th percentile of similar users (08:01) and closer to the bottom 5th percentile (05:35). You experience severe sleep restriction on workdays, averaging just 05:30 (6th percentile), compared to a much longer 07:43 on weekends (77th percentile). This massive fluctuation results in a sleep duration standard deviation of 02:39, placing you in the 99th percentile for duration variability.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC62887.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your sleep routine shows significant instability regarding when you wake up and how long you sleep. Your wake time standard deviation is high (01:50), placing you in the 81st percentile for variability. This inconsistency worsens on weekends, where the deviation reaches the 89th percentile. Consequently, your sleep duration is also highly volatile, with a standard deviation in the 87th percentile, indicating you rarely get the same amount of sleep two nights in a row.

### Sleep Quality
There is a drastic disparity in your sleep quality between workdays and weekends. Your average sleep score on workdays is poor (16th percentile), whereas your weekend sleep score is strong (75th percentile). On a positive note, your deep sleep duration is healthy, averaging 01:06 (72nd percentile). However, your REM sleep is slightly lower than average at 01:02 (34th percentile).

### Alertness
You recorded only one nap, but it was exceptionally long at 138 minutes. This duration is in the 97th percentile, suggesting a specific instance of severe exhaustion or recovery from acute sleep debt rather than a habitual napping routine.

### Timing
You exhibit a pattern of "social jetlag." While your average bedtimes are consistent between workdays (22:19) and weekends (22:24), your wake times shift dramatically. You wake up much earlier on workdays (05:09, 24th percentile) compared to weekends (06:55, 47th percentile). This causes your body's internal clock (midsleep point) to shift by nearly an hour later on weekends.

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is generally normal at 87.2% (54th percentile). You do not struggle significantly with sleep fragmentation; your average wake time after sleep onset is only roughly 11 minutes, which is in the 35th percentile (lower is better).

### Duration
You are likely chronically sleep-deprived during the workweek. Your average workday sleep duration is only 05:56, placing you in the 28th percentile. You clearly use the weekends to catch up on this debt, as your average weekend duration jumps to 07:31 (77th percentile). The gap between your shortest sleep (02:47) and longest sleep (09:29) is extreme.

SC64677.insights_output:
### Routine
Your overall bedtime standard deviation is 01:08 (53rd percentile), but your workday bedtime is highly consistent with a standard deviation of just 00:29 (15th percentile). Your wake time standard deviation is 01:10 (53rd percentile), and your midsleep point standard deviation is 01:01 (59th percentile), indicating moderate variability in your overall sleep schedule that stabilizes significantly during the workweek.

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep score is strong at 80.88 (78th percentile), driven by an excellent workday average of 82.55 (90th percentile). However, your weekend sleep score drops notably to 76.92 (37th percentile). Your sleep architecture shows unusually high REM sleep at 01:43 (89th percentile) and low light sleep at 03:51 (17th percentile), while deep sleep is normal at 01:13 (61st percentile). Your restlessness score is average at 0.081 (51st percentile). Your time to quality sleep is 00:21 (52nd percentile), but your wake time after sleep onset is slightly elevated at 13.70 minutes (76th percentile).

### Alertness
You do not take any naps, placing both your total number of naps and average nap length (0.0 minutes) in the 0th percentile. 

### Timing
You experience a significant shift in your sleep schedule on weekends, indicating social jetlag. Your workday bedtime is 22:30 (47th percentile), but it delays drastically to 00:37 on weekends (85th percentile). Consequently, your midsleep point shifts from 02:30 on workdays (49th percentile) to 04:22 on weekends (83rd percentile). Your wake time follows this pattern, shifting from 06:29 on workdays (50th percentile) to 08:07 on weekends (73rd percentile).

### Efficiency
Your average sleep efficiency is 87.1%, which is lower than most similar users (28th percentile). This reduced efficiency is largely driven by your wake time after sleep onset of 13.70 minutes (76th percentile), meaning you spend more time awake during the night than your peers. Your time to quality sleep remains average at 00:21 (52nd percentile).

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 06:49, which is slightly below average (39th percentile). Interestingly, you sleep significantly less on weekends, averaging only 06:24 (16th percentile), compared to a healthier 06:59 on workdays (55th percentile). Despite the drop on weekends, your overall sleep duration standard deviation is 00:54 (30th percentile), indicating that the length of your sleep is more consistent than most similar users.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC66498.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your consistency varies significantly between workdays and weekends. On workdays, your wake-up consistency is exceptional, with a standard deviation in the 1st percentile (very stable). However, your routine destabilizes on weekends; your average weekend bedtime (00:49) is nearly two hours later than your workday bedtime (23:10). This inconsistency suggests a "Social Jetlag" pattern where you live in two different time zones depending on the day of the week.

### Sleep Quality
Your sleep architecture regarding restorative stages is excellent. You achieve high amounts of Deep Sleep (1h 30m, 86th percentile) and REM Sleep (1h 45m, 95th percentile), which is vital for physical recovery and cognitive processing. However, your sleep continuity struggles. Your Wake After Sleep Onset averages nearly 18 minutes, placing you in the 97th percentile, meaning you spend significantly more time awake during the night than your peers.

### Alertness
You did not record any naps during this period (0th percentile).

### Timing
Your sleep timing reinforces the Social Jetlag noted in your routine. Your "midsleep point" (the middle of your sleep session) shifts from 03:26 on workdays to 05:24 on weekends. Consequently, your average wake time jumps from 07:42 on workdays to 09:59 on weekends (86th percentile). This 2+ hour shift can confuse your circadian rhythm and make Monday mornings particularly difficult.

### Efficiency
Sleep efficiency is your most significant area for improvement. Your average efficiency is 83.8%, which falls in the 2nd percentile, meaning 98% of similar users have better sleep efficiency. This low score is driven by the time you spend awake during the night and a long "Time to Quality Sleep" (38 minutes), which is in the 95th percentile. You are spending a long time in bed trying to settle into stable sleep.

### Duration
You are getting a healthy amount of total sleep. Your average duration is 7h 17m, placing you in the 81st percentile. While you sleep longer on weekends (7h 48m) than workdays (7h 05m), both durations are sufficient for an adult male under 30. Your duration standard deviation is in the 21st percentile, indicating that while your timing shifts, the actual amount of sleep you get remains fairly consistent.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC68292.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your bedtime consistency during the workweek is highly irregular. Your workday bedtime standard deviation is 01:40, placing you in the 78th percentile for variability. While your weekend routine is more stable (37th percentile), this drastic fluctuation during the week prevents your body from establishing a strong rhythm.

### Sleep Quality
Your REM sleep duration is critically low. You average only 58 minutes of REM sleep, placing you in the 5th percentile compared to similar users. Consequently, your overall Sleep Score is in the 10th percentile. Additionally, your sleep is fragmented; your average time spent awake after sleep onset is 14.6 minutes, which is high (84th percentile). You are spending a disproportionate amount of time in Light Sleep (81st percentile) rather than restorative stages.

### Alertness
You engage in very long naps, averaging 93 minutes (72nd percentile). While you only recorded two naps, their excessive length suggests you are using them to recover from significant acute sleep debt rather than for a quick alertness boost.

### Timing
You naturally favor an early schedule, with an average bedtime of 21:46 (10th percentile) and a wake time of 05:58 (9th percentile). However, you occasionally push your bedtime extremely late (e.g., 02:36), which conflicts with your natural tendency to wake up early, resulting in truncated rest on those specific nights.

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is 86.7%, falling into the 22nd percentile. This lower efficiency is primarily driven by your high Wake After Sleep Onset (84th percentile), meaning you struggle to stay asleep once you have drifted off, rather than struggling to fall asleep initially (latency is normal at the 30th percentile).

### Duration
Your sleep duration is highly inconsistent. The standard deviation of your sleep duration is in the 71st percentile. There is a distinct "social jetlag" pattern where you sleep significantly less on workdays (average 06:48, 35th percentile) compared to weekends (average 07:47, 65th percentile). You are relying on weekends to catch up on lost sleep from the week.

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC69748.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your sleep schedule shows significant inconsistency, particularly regarding when you go to bed during the work week. Your workday bedtime standard deviation is in the **84th percentile**, indicating your bedtime fluctuates much more than most women your age. Similarly, your overall sleep duration varies significantly day-to-day (standard deviation in the **79th percentile**), suggesting a lack of a stable rhythm.

### Sleep Quality
Your sleep architecture shows a concerning imbalance. Your average REM sleep duration is extremely low, sitting in the **1st percentile** (averaging only 42 minutes). Conversely, your light sleep is very high, in the **90th percentile**. This lack of restorative REM sleep is likely the primary driver of your low overall sleep score, which falls in the **9th percentile**.

### Alertness
You generally do not rely on naps, recording only one nap in this period. However, that single nap was 122 minutes long (**94th percentile**), which suggests that when you do reach a point of exhaustion, you require a significant amount of daytime recovery sleep to compensate.

### Timing
You have an "early bird" chronotype, operating much earlier than your peers. Your average bedtime (20:39) is in the **3rd percentile**, and your average wake time (04:53) is in the **6th percentile**. Your midsleep point (00:46) is also in the **3rd percentile**, confirming a significantly advanced sleep phase compared to similar users.

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is lower than expected, ranking in the **12th percentile** (85.7%). While your time to fall asleep is average, you spend a notable amount of time awake after sleep onset (average ~15 minutes, **78th percentile**), which fragments your rest and lowers your overall efficiency.

### Duration
While your average sleep duration of 7 hours and 3 minutes is healthy (**61st percentile**), this average masks extreme volatility. You oscillate between severe sleep deprivation (e.g., 3 hours 22 minutes on Day 7) and hypersomnia (e.g., 9 hours 26 minutes on Day 3). This "yo-yo" pattern prevents your body from establishing a consistent recovery baseline.

SC73082.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly irregular, particularly during the workweek. Your overall wake time standard deviation is 1 hour and 32 minutes (72nd percentile), showing significant day-to-day fluctuation on both workdays (76th percentile) and weekends (78th percentile). Bedtime variability is also high on workdays with a standard deviation of 1 hour and 29 minutes (77th percentile). However, your weekend bedtimes are surprisingly consistent, with a standard deviation of just 38 minutes (19th percentile). Overall, your midsleep point standard deviation is elevated at 1 hour and 11 minutes (69th percentile).

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep quality is poor, reflected by an average sleep score of 72.31 (10th percentile). This is heavily impacted by a severe lack of REM sleep, which averages just 56 minutes (7th percentile), and high nighttime restlessness (85th percentile). Your light sleep duration is also quite low at 3 hours and 42 minutes (11th percentile). On a positive note, your deep sleep duration is healthy and normal at 1 hour and 12 minutes (55th percentile). You transition into quality sleep very quickly, taking only 13 minutes (10th percentile), and your wake time after sleep onset is average at 11.52 minutes (51st percentile).

### Alertness
You do not take any naps to supplement your short nighttime sleep, placing both your nap frequency and average nap duration (0.0 minutes) in the 0th percentile. 

### Timing
Your sleep timing is characterized by average-to-late bedtimes paired with very early wake times. Your average bedtime is 23:14 (66th percentile), shifting later to 23:48 on weekends (69th percentile) compared to 23:01 on workdays (64th percentile). Your average wake time is early at 05:57 (19th percentile). You wake up about an hour later on weekends (06:43, 24th percentile) than you do on workdays (05:39, 18th percentile). Your average midsleep point remains relatively central at 02:36 (44th percentile).

### Efficiency
Your overall sleep efficiency is below average at 87.2% (29th percentile) compared to similar users. Interestingly, the metrics that typically drag down efficiency are normal or excellent for you: your wake time after sleep onset is perfectly average at 11.52 minutes (51st percentile), and your time to quality sleep is exceptionally fast at 13 minutes (10th percentile). Your reduced efficiency is likely driven by your high restlessness rather than prolonged periods of being awake in bed.

### Duration
Your sleep duration is critically low and highly inconsistent. You average only 5 hours and 51 minutes of sleep per night, placing you in the bottom 6th percentile of similar users. This severe sleep restriction is present throughout the entire week; you average 5 hours and 47 minutes on workdays (8th percentile) and only slightly more at 6 hours on weekends (8th percentile). Furthermore, your nightly sleep duration fluctuates drastically, with a standard deviation of 1 hour and 26 minutes (73rd percentile).

SC75171.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule shows notable inconsistency, particularly when going to sleep. Your bedtime standard deviation is 01:37 (71st percentile), which worsens slightly on workdays to 01:39 (79th percentile). Your wake time routine is much more stable, with a standard deviation of 01:13 (48th percentile). Your midsleep point standard deviation sits at 01:19 (67th percentile), further reflecting the day-to-day shifts driven primarily by your fluctuating bedtimes.

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep score averages 74.55 (26th percentile), indicating below-average sleep quality. The most critical out-of-range metric is your deep sleep duration, which averages only 00:42 (3rd percentile)—falling below the bottom 5th percentile (00:45) of similar individuals. Light sleep is also low at 03:44 (17th percentile), while REM sleep is normal at 01:17 (53rd percentile). Additionally, your sleep is highly disturbed; your average restlessness score is 0.128 (89th percentile). It takes you 00:30 to reach quality sleep (85th percentile), though your wake time after sleep onset remains relatively low at 9.19 minutes (23rd percentile).

### Alertness
You recorded 0 total naps, resulting in an average nap length of 0.0 minutes (0th percentile). 

### Timing
Your sleep timing is heavily delayed, indicating a strong night-owl profile. Your average bedtime is 01:58 (93rd percentile). This shifts even later on weekends to 02:23 (91st percentile) compared to 01:48 on workdays (93rd percentile). Consequently, your average wake time is 08:35 (86th percentile), pushing to 09:31 on weekends (84th percentile) versus 08:12 on workdays (84th percentile). Your average midsleep point is 05:16 (91st percentile), remaining consistently late across both workdays (05:00, 90th percentile) and weekends (05:57, 90th percentile).

### Efficiency
Your average sleep efficiency is 86.9% (36th percentile), placing you slightly below the median of similar individuals (whose typical range is 84.4% to 89.9%). While your wake time after sleep onset is better than most at just 9.19 minutes (23rd percentile), your overall efficiency is negatively impacted by your prolonged time to quality sleep of 00:30 (85th percentile), which is significantly longer than the bottom 5th percentile (00:12) of your peers.

### Duration
You are experiencing severe sleep restriction. Your average sleep duration is only 05:44 (8th percentile), which is drastically lower than the top 95th percentile (07:48) of similar individuals. This deprivation is most prominent on workdays, where you average just 05:34 (8th percentile). You attempt to recover slightly on weekends, extending your sleep to 06:08, though this still leaves you in the low 18th percentile. Your sleep duration standard deviation is 01:00 (41st percentile), indicating moderate day-to-day variability in how long you sleep.

SC79011.insights_output:
### Routine
Your bedtime is highly consistent, with a standard deviation of just 46 minutes (22nd percentile). However, your wake time is quite inconsistent, showing a standard deviation of 1 hour and 31 minutes (70th percentile). This discrepancy results in a moderately variable midsleep point standard deviation of 1 hour and 2 minutes (56th percentile).

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep score is below average at 75.89 (34th percentile), and it drops even lower on workdays to 74.13 (23rd percentile). This lower quality is driven by a very high light sleep duration of 5 hours and 14 minutes (94th percentile) and a low REM sleep duration of just 58 minutes (23rd percentile). Furthermore, your sleep is highly disrupted: your restlessness score is 0.117 (84th percentile), and your wake time after sleep onset is exceptionally high at 19.92 minutes (98th percentile). Your deep sleep duration remains normal at 1 hour and 6 minutes (60th percentile). 

### Alertness
You did not take any naps during this period, placing your total nap count and average nap length at 0 minutes (0th percentile).

### Timing
You maintain an early average bedtime of 21:53 (17th percentile). This early bedtime is consistent across both workdays (21:49, 20th percentile) and weekends (22:04, 14th percentile). Your average wake time is 06:30 (52nd percentile), but there is a noticeable shift between workdays (06:05, 48th percentile) and weekends, where you sleep in until 07:31 (59th percentile). Your average midsleep point leans early at 02:12 (30th percentile).

### Efficiency
Your average sleep efficiency is very low at 85.1% (12th percentile) compared to similar users. This poor efficiency is heavily impacted by your extremely high wake time after sleep onset of 19.92 minutes (98th percentile) and a slightly elevated time to quality sleep of 22 minutes (67th percentile). 

### Duration
Your sleep duration is excellent compared to similar users. You average 7 hours and 19 minutes of sleep overall (87th percentile). You maintain strong sleep durations on workdays at 7 hours and 1 minute (79th percentile) and extend this significantly on weekends to 8 hours and 4 minutes (91st percentile). Your sleep duration standard deviation is 1 hour and 3 minutes (45th percentile), indicating average day-to-day variability in how long you sleep.

SC80016.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly consistent. Your bedtime standard deviation is just 29 minutes (10th percentile), your wake time standard deviation is 34 minutes (20th percentile), and your midsleep point standard deviation is 24 minutes (11th percentile). This indicates excellent adherence to a regular daily routine.

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep score is strong at 80.80 (73rd percentile). You experience very low restlessness with a score of 0.060 (15th percentile) and transition to quality sleep quickly in just 12 minutes (9th percentile). Your wake time after sleep onset is also exceptionally low at 10.18 minutes (10th percentile). Regarding sleep architecture, your light sleep duration of 4 hours 52 minutes (59th percentile) and REM sleep duration of 1 hour 5 minutes (37th percentile) are normal. However, your deep sleep duration is slightly on the lower end at 42 minutes (21st percentile).

### Alertness
You took zero naps during this logging period, placing your total nap count and average nap length in the 0th percentile. 

### Timing
Your sleep timing is significantly earlier than most people in your age group, indicating a pronounced advanced sleep phase. Your average bedtime is 21:01 (6th percentile), your average wake time is 04:33 (3rd percentile), and your midsleep point is 00:47 (3rd percentile). This early pattern persists throughout the week, though your timing shifts slightly later on weekends (average weekend bedtime of 21:08 vs. workday 20:59; weekend wake time of 04:51 vs. workday 04:25). 

### Efficiency
Your sleep is highly efficient compared to similar users. Your overall sleep efficiency is 88.7% (80th percentile). This high efficiency is driven by a very brief wake time after sleep onset of 10.18 minutes (10th percentile, closely approaching the bottom 5% threshold of 9.02 minutes for similar users) and a fast time to quality sleep of 12 minutes (9th percentile, nearly matching the bottom 5% mark of 11 minutes).

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 6 hours and 40 minutes, which is typical for your demographic (42nd percentile). You sleep slightly longer on weekends (6 hours 51 minutes, 46th percentile) compared to workdays (6 hours 35 minutes, 39th percentile). Your sleep duration is highly stable night-to-night, with a standard deviation of just 38 minutes (12th percentile).

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. When determining whether a metric is normal or abnormal, always provide the corresponding percentile. Avoid generic statements. Avoid incorrect knowledge, inconsistencies and contradictions. Don't mention "the user". Talk like you're speaking directly to someone. Be concise. # Sleep insights report
SC83845.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly irregular. The standard deviations for your bedtime (8 hours and 8 minutes), wake time (9 hours and 10 minutes), and midsleep point (8 hours and 34 minutes) are all exceptionally high, placing you in the 99th percentile for all three metrics. 

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep score is below average at 76.93 (39th percentile), and it drops significantly on workdays to 75.14 (26th percentile). You take longer than most to reach quality sleep, averaging 27 minutes (83rd percentile). However, your sleep architecture is relatively healthy: your REM sleep averages 1 hour and 31 minutes (78th percentile), deep sleep is 1 hour and 21 minutes (58th percentile), and light sleep is 4 hours and 5 minutes (40th percentile). Your restlessness score is average at 0.096 (52nd percentile), and your wake time after sleep onset is low at 10.20 minutes (33rd percentile).

### Alertness
You only recorded one nap, but its duration was extremely long at 175 minutes, placing you in the 99th percentile. A nap of this length is highly abnormal and can severely disrupt your nighttime sleep drive.

### Timing
Your sleep timing is significantly delayed and shifts drastically between workdays and weekends, indicating severe social jetlag. Your overall average bedtime is 01:51 (78th percentile) and your average wake time is 09:45 (81st percentile). On weekends, your schedule shifts extremely late: your average weekend bedtime is 04:50 (97th percentile), your weekend wake time is 13:26 (97th percentile), and your weekend midsleep point is 09:08 (97th percentile). This contrasts sharply with your workday bedtime of 00:36 (60th percentile) and workday wake time of 08:11 (58th percentile).

### Efficiency
Your overall sleep efficiency is 87.7%, which is slightly above average (61st percentile compared to similar users). Your wake time after sleep onset is relatively low at 10.20 minutes (33rd percentile), meaning you stay asleep well once you fall asleep. However, your time to quality sleep is prolonged at 27 minutes (83rd percentile compared to similar users), indicating initial difficulty settling into restorative sleep stages.

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 6 hours and 57 minutes (60th percentile compared to similar users). However, your day-to-day sleep duration is highly inconsistent, with a standard deviation of 2 hours and 21 minutes (96th percentile). You are relying on weekends to catch up on sleep debt, averaging 7 hours and 32 minutes on weekends (78th percentile) compared to just 6 hours and 43 minutes on workdays (48th percentile).

Optionally (only do this if extremely important) add a heading called Other for anything else that doesn't fit the above categories. - For Routine, consider the average bedtime, wake time, midsleep point and standard deviations of these, focus on the consistency of the routine, not timing. - For Sleep Quality, consider light sleep duration, deep sleep duration, REM sleep duration, sleep score, restlessness score, time to quality sleep, and wake time after sleep onset. - For Alertness, consider the number of naps and nap length. - For Timing, consider midsleep point, bedtime, wake time, make any comments on weekend vs. workday. - For Efficiency, consider sleep efficiency, wake time after sleep onset, and time to quality sleep, describe how they compare to similar users. - For Duration, consider average sleep duration, weekend vs. workday sleep durations and standard deviations, describe how they compare to similar users. SC86935.insights_output:
### Routine
*   Your bedtime is highly consistent, with a standard deviation of just 55 minutes (25th percentile). This consistency is maintained across both workdays (26th percentile) and weekends (20th percentile).
*   However, your wake times are much less consistent. Your overall wake time standard deviation is 1 hour and 32 minutes (57th percentile), driven largely by high variability on workdays (71st percentile). 
*   Your midsleep point standard deviation is 59 minutes (38th percentile), indicating a relatively stable sleep window despite the fluctuating wake times.

### Sleep Quality
*   **Extremely low REM sleep:** You average only 43 minutes of REM sleep per night, placing you in the 2nd percentile. For context, the bottom 5% of similar users still average at least 49 minutes.
*   **Low deep sleep:** Your average deep sleep duration is 1 hour and 6 minutes, which is below average (25th percentile).
*   **High light sleep:** Because your restorative sleep stages are lacking, your light sleep is unusually high at 4 hours and 51 minutes (86th percentile).
*   **High restlessness:** Your average restlessness score is 0.128, placing you in the 88th percentile, indicating highly fragmented or physically restless sleep.
*   Consequently, your overall average sleep score is 76.94, which is below average (40th percentile).

### Alertness
*   You rarely rely on daytime sleep, recording only 1 nap during this period.
*   The duration of this nap was 56 minutes, which is typical and sits in the 54th percentile compared to similar users.

### Timing
*   Your entire sleep schedule is significantly delayed compared to your peers. Your average bedtime is 01:34 (84th percentile).
*   This delay is exacerbated on weekends, where your average bedtime shifts to 02:31 (88th percentile), compared to 01:11 on workdays (82nd percentile).
*   Your average wake time is correspondingly late at 09:16 (85th percentile), pushing back to 10:04 on weekends (84th percentile).
*   As a result, your average midsleep point is 05:25 (86th percentile), indicating a strong "night owl" chronotype or delayed sleep phase.

### Efficiency
*   Your average sleep efficiency is 86.7%, which is below average (35th percentile). 
*   Despite the lower overall efficiency, your time to quality sleep (21 minutes, 53rd percentile) and wake time after sleep onset (11.09 minutes, 52nd percentile) are both perfectly average compared to similar users. This suggests your reduced efficiency is likely tied to your high restlessness rather than prolonged awakenings or difficulty falling asleep.

### Duration
*   Your average sleep duration is 6 hours and 41 minutes, which is slightly below average (43rd percentile).
*   Interestingly, you sleep slightly less on weekends (6 hours and 37 minutes, 34th percentile) than on workdays (6 hours and 42 minutes, 50th percentile).
*   Your sleep duration fluctuates significantly from night to night, with a standard deviation of 1 hour and 26 minutes (71st percentile). This indicates that while your bedtime is consistent, your varying wake times are causing highly unpredictable total sleep amounts.

SC87916.insights_output:
### Routine
* Your bedtime fluctuates significantly, with a standard deviation of 01:30 (78th percentile). This inconsistency is even more pronounced on weekends, where your bedtime standard deviation jumps to 01:59 (89th percentile).
* Your wake time is much more stable, showing a standard deviation of 00:58 (52nd percentile).
* Your midsleep point standard deviation is 00:58 (69th percentile), reflecting the downstream impact of your highly variable bedtimes.

### Sleep Quality
* **Out of range:** Your average deep sleep duration is notably low at just 00:28, placing you in the 9th percentile compared to similar users. 
* Your REM sleep (01:04, 65th percentile) and light sleep (04:49, 42nd percentile) are within normal ranges.
* Despite the lack of deep sleep, your overall sleep score is strong at 80.53 (74th percentile), and your restlessness score is low at 0.075 (29th percentile).

### Alertness
* You recorded 0 naps during this period, placing your nap frequency and average nap length in the 0th percentile. 

### Timing
* Your average bedtime is 23:52 (81st percentile), which is relatively late for your demographic. On weekends, your average bedtime shifts even later to 00:02.
* Your average wake time is 07:13 (69th percentile), remaining highly consistent between workdays (07:12) and weekends (07:15).
* Your average midsleep point is 03:33 (78th percentile), which aligns with your later-than-average sleep schedule.

### Efficiency
* Your average sleep efficiency is 86.9%, placing you in the 53rd percentile, which is perfectly average compared to similar users.
* You transition into quality sleep very quickly, averaging just 11 minutes (17th percentile).
* Your wake time after sleep onset is 13.74 minutes (41st percentile), indicating you do not spend excessive time awake during the night once you fall asleep.

### Duration
* Your average sleep duration is 06:22, placing you in the 35th percentile, which is slightly below average for your age group.
* You sleep slightly less on weekends (06:15, 27th percentile) compared to workdays (06:25, 39th percentile).
* **Out of range:** Your sleep duration varies considerably from night to night, with a standard deviation of 01:23 (75th percentile).

SC90880.insights_output:
### Routine
Your sleep schedule shows strong consistency, particularly in the mornings. Your wake time standard deviation is just 30 minutes (12th percentile), indicating a highly stable wake routine. Your bedtime fluctuates slightly more, with a standard deviation of 50 minutes (40th percentile). Overall, your midsleep point standard deviation is 32 minutes (25th percentile), reflecting a well-maintained and consistent sleep routine.

### Sleep Quality
Your average sleep score is 76.50 (40th percentile). Your sleep architecture shows lower-than-average durations in specific stages: light sleep averages 03:56 (12th percentile) and REM sleep averages 00:51 (26th percentile). Deep sleep is closer to average at 00:45 (37th percentile). On a positive note, your sleep is very calm, with a low restlessness score of 0.068 (17th percentile). You also transition into quality sleep quickly, taking only 11 minutes (15th percentile), and experience minimal disruptions, with a wake time after sleep onset (WASO) of just 11.23 minutes (21st percentile).

### Alertness
You recorded a total of 2 naps during this period. Your average nap duration is 52.0 minutes, which places you exactly in the 49th percentile. 

### Timing
Your sleep timing is shifted significantly earlier than average. Your overall bedtime is 21:57 (24th percentile), and your wake time is extremely early at 04:23 (4th percentile). Consequently, your average midsleep point is 01:10 (9th percentile). This early-bird pattern persists throughout the week: workday wake times average 04:17 (6th percentile) with bedtimes at 22:00 (27th percentile), while weekend wake times average 04:35 (4th percentile) with bedtimes at 21:51 (19th percentile).

### Efficiency
Your average sleep efficiency is 86.7% (49th percentile), placing you right in the middle compared to similar individuals. However, your specific efficiency metrics are better than most: your time to quality sleep is rapid at 11 minutes (15th percentile), and your wake time after sleep onset is very low at 11.23 minutes (21st percentile). This indicates that while your overall efficiency is average, you fall asleep quickly and experience very few awakenings during the night compared to your peers.

### Duration
Your sleep duration is critically low and falls well out of the preferred range. You average just 05:33 of sleep per night (10th percentile compared to similar individuals). This deficit is more pronounced on workdays, where you average 05:25 (9th percentile), with a slight recovery on weekends to 05:53 (16th percentile). Your sleep duration standard deviation is 45 minutes (23rd percentile), showing that your sleep length is consistently short rather than highly variable.

SC92149.insights_output:
# Sleep insights report

### Routine
Your sleep schedule is highly consistent. Your bedtime standard deviation is 38 minutes (12th percentile), indicating very little fluctuation in when you go to sleep. Your wake time standard deviation is 58 minutes (34th percentile), and your midsleep point standard deviation is 39 minutes (22nd percentile). Overall, you maintain a very stable day-to-day sleep routine.

### Sleep Quality
Your overall sleep score is 77.88 (52nd percentile), though it drops on workdays to 76.53 (42nd percentile) compared to a better 81.17 (71st percentile) on weekends. Your light sleep averages 4 hours and 53 minutes (79th percentile), and your deep sleep is healthy at 1 hour and 10 minutes (76th percentile). However, your REM sleep is slightly below average at 1 hour and 7 minutes (41st percentile). 

Several metrics fall out of the preferred range and indicate fragmented sleep: your restlessness score is high at 0.119 (83rd percentile), your time to quality sleep is prolonged at 28 minutes (87th percentile), and your wake time after sleep onset is elevated at 17.64 minutes (92nd percentile). 

### Alertness
You recorded a total of 2 naps during this period. Notably out of the preferred range is your average nap length of 89.5 minutes (82nd percentile). Naps of this duration can reduce your nighttime sleep drive and are likely contributing to your prolonged time to quality sleep at night.

### Timing
Your average bedtime is 22:57 (56th percentile), shifting only slightly from 22:52 on workdays to 23:09 on weekends. Your average wake time is 07:24 (83rd percentile), with a more noticeable shift between workdays (07:09) and weekends (08:00). Consequently, your average midsleep point is 03:10 (71st percentile), moving from 03:00 on workdays to 03:35 on weekends.

### Efficiency
Your sleep efficiency is out of the preferred range at 85.1% (13th percentile), which is notably lower than similar users (the bottom 5th percentile for your demographic is 83.8%). This reduced efficiency is directly driven by your high wake time after sleep onset of 17.64 minutes (92nd percentile, nearing the top 95th percentile of 18.27 minutes for similar users) and a prolonged time to quality sleep of 28 minutes (87th percentile, approaching the top 95th percentile of 33 minutes). 

### Duration
Your average sleep duration is 7 hours and 11 minutes (81st percentile), which is well above average compared to similar users (the top 95th percentile is 7 hours and 41 minutes). You sleep longer on weekends, averaging 7 hours and 45 minutes (82nd percentile), compared to 6 hours and 57 minutes on workdays (76th percentile). Your sleep duration standard deviation is 55 minutes (30th percentile), showing that the total amount of sleep you get remains relatively consistent from day to day.
